title: Stop at the Wall
text:
Where we left off



Last week
What you own




📦 my_lib.py begins
drive_straight · turn_to


📏 measured, not guessed
DEG_PER_MM, rolled & measured


🔁 replay a plan
drive_square(300) snaps shut




🤔 But a script is blind — today’s new job: sense → decide → act.

⏱️ Time: 5 minutes — then a bug hunt

Recap by asking, not telling: “what does drive_straight(300) know about the world?” Nothing — it counts wheel-degrees (healing its heading on the way) and stops. That’s replay. Today’s whole arc is the opposite: the robot reads something live, then chooses what to do. Three new powers land today — the motor as a sensor (stall = touch), the color eye (reflection = the echo of its own light), and if/else (the first real decision) — then we wrap them in a loop so one decision becomes a patrol, and the library gains its first sensing verbs. Keep this slide to five minutes: per the W2 delivery rhythm (decision #22), robots are out by minute 15, so the recap is a springboard, not a lecture.

title: Stop at the Wall
section: 🔎 Bug hunt: the tune that didn’t stick
text:
🔎 Bug hunt: the tune that didn’t stick
def make_it_sharper():
    KP = 12          # crank steering for the whole library… right?

make_it_sharper()
drive_straight(300)  # …still lazy. Nothing changed. 😤

Watch it stay lazy — find the bug. Where should KP = 12 live?



Inside a def you made a local — dead the moment it returned. Change globals only at the top.



This closes Day 5’s leftover (globals/shadowing), delivered as a live hunt instead of slides — decision #22. Run the planted bug on the coach robot and let squads diagnose for ~5 minutes before any reveal; the 😤 must be theirs. Two live moments: (1) the fix — move KP = 12 to the constants block, rerun, the straight sharpens; (2) print(error) after a run → NameError, proof that locals evaporate (and that’s a feature: every verb gets private scratch names). Close with the D5 verbal pass, squad by squad, in their own words: default = factory setting · keyword = one-call override · top of file = global · inside a def = local. Do NOT teach the global keyword — the constants-block discipline is strictly better. Coach’s radar for W2–3: “I tuned it and it didn’t stick” → first question is where did you assign it?

title: Stop at the Wall
section: 🤖 Robots out: Bullseye on the real mat 🎯
text:
🤖 Robots out: Bullseye on the real mat 🎯

🎯 drive_straight(1000) on the real mat — one attempt, ±2 cm passes
📦 drive_square(300) — watch it snap shut where it started
⏱️ 15 minutes, then the robot gets its sense of touch


Pure energy burner with a hidden agenda: it closes the remaining D5 gate legs (drive_straight 1.0 m ±2 cm, square closed) on the real surface, and it front-loads the session with driving instead of sitting — the drill-first rhythm (decision #22). Logistics: mat on the ground, no table yet (decision #23) — the wall board goes at the mat edge for the stall work later; nothing today needs borders at true positions. Watch for surprises vs the tape field: the print’s friction and colour differ — if someone’s 1 m lands long/short vs last week, that’s the calibration-transfer checkpoint doing its job (re-measure, don’t panic). Keep it to 15 minutes; the leaderboard can wait for Gentle Giant.

title: Stop at the Wall
text:
Feel it: see the invisible 📉
# Run this ALONE first — watch the number, not the robot.
while True:
    print("wheel speed:", left.speed())
    wait(100)
🤖 Robots out. Run the loop, grab the wheels — watch it dive. Then the wall board: same dive, no hand.


The speed dive is the wall — every motor has stalled() and run_until_stalled().



“Make the invisible visible” — same habit as the gyro heading in Week 1, and this one happens on the real robot, not a slide (the animated version lives in the 🏠 appendix for home replay). Sequence: print loop running → student hand-blocks the wheels → speed crashes → then the same crash against the wall board. Only after they’ve seen the crash do you name the stall tools: left.stalled() asks “are you jammed right now?”, left.run_until_stalled() is “roll until you jam” in one call. Point out why this beats an ultrasonic sensor even if we had a free port: a touch via stall is repeatable to the millimetre and never lies about a wall it can “see through.”

title: Stop at the Wall
section: Fix it: stall = touch, then back off 🧱
text:
Fix it: stall = touch, then back off 🧱
APPROACH_SPEED = 150         # deg/s — gentle, not a crash
right.run(APPROACH_SPEED)    # right rolls along…
left.run_until_stalled(APPROACH_SPEED,
                       duty_limit=50)   # …until LEFT jams
right.stop()
hub.speaker.beep()           # "I arrived"
drive_straight(-BACKOFF_MM)         # measured, repeatable
🤖 Robots out. Build it, tune BACKOFF_MM against the board — this is today’s competition in seven lines.


Gentle touch · wait for the jam · back off a measured BACKOFF_MM.



Stress the gentleness knobs: a low approach speed (≈150 deg/s) and duty_limit=50 — the duty limit caps how hard the motor is allowed to push, so the reading is crisp and the LEGO survives. Driving straight into a wall jams both wheels, so watching ONE motor is enough — run_until_stalled blocks on the left while the right just rolls. Line 6 calls their own drive_straight with a negative distance — the challenge parked at the library day is now urgent: flip the while comparison (or mirror it for negatives) in my_lib.py, two-minute fix, big payoff. Back-off is measured, not guessed — BACKOFF_MM = 50 ends exactly 5 cm off the wall, every run. (The pro equivalent, DriveBase.stalled(), arrives with the D10 reveal.)

title: Stop at the Wall
text:
Meet the eye 🔦
A flashlight + light meter in one brick — it measures its own echo.

  
    
      
      
      
    
    
    reflection() → 10
    reflection() → 88
    
    
      
      
    
    
      
      
    
    
    
    
    
    
    
    
    
    
    black line — echo swallowed
    white mat — echo bounces back
    same flashlight —
    different echo
  

left_eye.reflection()   # 0-100: % of its OWN light echoing back
left_eye.color()        # a name: Color.RED... (Week 4's tool)


Anything that changes the echo changes the number — ✨ gloss · 💡 light · 📏 height.



The live demo beats the diagram: run a print(left_eye.reflection()) loop, hold the robot, and let students slide surfaces under the eye, tilt it, lift it — the number dances. Establish the active-sensor idea: it brings its own LED, so it mostly ignores the room, but gloss, sensor height, and strong ambient light still shift the echo — which is exactly why every surface gets its own calibration (next slide). reflection() vs color(): today reflection does all the work; color() names colors and gets one cameo (the RED beep) — its real job is Week 4’s notes. Two eyes fill ports B and F; today one eye is plenty — D7 explains why we carry two.

title: Stop at the Wall
section: Break it: the threshold you guessed 🔥
text:
Break it: the threshold you guessed 🔥

Last week the tape split at 50 (white 88, black 10). Does 50 still work on the mat? The floor?

🤖 Robots out. Print reflection() across three surfaces — tape · mat · floor. Log white & black for each.


A threshold is a fact about a surface — read white · read black · split the middle.



Let each squad predict before measuring — commitments make the surprise land. The printed mat just arrived (decision #23), so this is also the real calibration-transfer checkpoint from the plan: the numbers they log here ARE the re-logged thresholds. Expected outcome: the mat’s white and the tape-field’s white read differently (print ink vs paper, matte tarp vs tape), the split point moves, and the habit lands — look first, then split what you actually see. The glossy-table failure (black reading 60, < 50 calling the line “clear” and driving over it) is the venue-day version of the same lesson; it lives as an interactive sim in the 🏠 appendix since we don’t have a glossy table in the room — mention it, don’t show it. The takeaway is a habit, not a number: re-read and re-split, every surface.

title: Stop at the Wall
section: Fix it: calibrate, then decide — if / else 🎚️
text:
Fix it: calibrate, then decide — if / else 🎚️
# Calibrate FIRST — measure, never guess
REFLECT_THRESHOLD = 50              # for THIS surface
while True:
    if left_eye.color() == Color.RED:
        hub.speaker.beep(frequency=880)     # high beep on red
    elif left_eye.reflection() < REFLECT_THRESHOLD:
        hub.speaker.beep(frequency=220)     # low beep on a line
    wait(50)
🤖 Robots out. Threshold from your mat numbers — low beep on lines, high beep on red.


if … elif is the robot choosing — new surface → re-calibrate the threshold, never the code.



This is the just-in-time concept for the day: if/elif + booleans. Keep it concrete — a boolean is the answer to a yes/no question the robot asks the world (color() == RED, reflection() < 50). elif = “otherwise, check this next.” Tie it straight back to the three-surface ritual: the threshold is data you recalibrate per surface; the if/elif structure never changes. The mat’s printed staff lines make the beeper test real mission geography — they’re the same lines D7 follows and squares on. If a squad is flying, mention else for the catch-all case, but don’t gate on it.

title: Stop at the Wall
text:
Break it: the one-shot miss 😴

Your sense→act runs once. What happens at the second wall?

🤖 Robots out. Run it, aim it at a second wall by hand. Nothing.


Your code was right — it just stopped too soon. That’s a loop’s job.



Let them run the one-shot to death on the real robot: it handles wall 1 perfectly, then dies — perfection that only lasts one event. The point is emotional before it’s technical: “your code was right — it just stopped too soon.” Loops are not a new trick from scratch; they already met while and for on Day 3’s square. Today those loops wrap a sensed decision instead of a fixed count of sides. (The animated one-shot-vs-patrol comparison lives in the 🏠 appendix.)

title: Stop at the Wall
text:
Name it: the patrol loop 🔁
hub.imu.reset_heading(0)
for lap in range(PATROL_LAPS):          # counted repeat = laps
    left.run(PATROL_SPEED); right.run(PATROL_SPEED)
    while not left.stalled():           # repeat until the wall
        if left_eye.color() == Color.RED:
            hub.speaker.beep(880)       # react, don't stop
        wait(10)
    left.stop(); right.stop()
    drive_straight(-BACKOFF_MM)                # measured back-off
    turn_to(90 * (lap + 1))             # absolute appointment


Two loops nested — for counts laps, while waits for the wall, the if reacts on the move.



Read the shape aloud: outer for = “do this lap N times,” inner while not left.stalled() = “keep rolling until you feel the wall,” inner if = “and if you spot red on the way, sing out.” The if living inside the while is the subtle win — reaction happens continuously during motion, not as a stop-and-check. (That’s also why this loop uses left.stalled() rather than the blocking run_until_stalled from this morning — the robot needs to keep checking things while it rolls.) turn_to(90 * (lap + 1)) is the library day’s absolute-appointment trick doing its job silently: corner 1 aims at 90, corner 2 at 180 — a bumped corner can’t poison the next one. This program is the literal answer key for Gentle Giant’s loop bonus. With one board and no table borders yet, patrol two “walls” by moving the board between reps or using a wall + heavy box pair — the loop doesn’t care.

title: Stop at the Wall
section: drive_to_wall and wall_square join the library 📦
text:
drive_to_wall and wall_square join the library 📦
def drive_to_wall(speed=150):
    """Roll gently until the wheels feel the wall."""
    right.run(speed)
    left.run_until_stalled(speed, duty_limit=50)
    right.stop()

def wall_square(heading=0):
    """Press BOTH wheels flat on the wall → truth restored."""
    left.run(120); right.run(120)
    while not (left.stalled() and right.stalled()):
        wait(10)
    left.stop(); right.stop()
    hub.imu.reset_heading(heading)   # the wall never lies
    drive_straight(-BACKOFF_MM)


Both wheels flat on the wall → heading known again. The wall never lies.



Same promotion ritual as the library day: the morning’s loose fix-it lines get names and move into my_lib.py, right under drive_straight and turn_to. Walk wall_square slowly — it’s the first verb whose output is trust, not motion: waiting for BOTH motors to stall means the bumper is flat on the wall, so the physical world just told you your heading exactly; reset_heading(heading) writes that truth back into the gyro, and the measured back-off leaves you at a known distance too. Gyro drift accumulates over a 2-minute run — squaring is how Weeks 3–4 missions reset it between tasks, which is why this humble verb outlives almost everything else. Demo: spin the robot a few times by hand (drift injected), wall_square, then a turn_to(90) — still true. Table note (decision #23): until the bordered table exists, wall_square trains on the fixed board; when the table lands we re-validate it at the true border positions — that’s the table-arrival checkpoint.

Read the scoresheet: anchor first 🗺️

  
    ▶ build the score, layer by layer
    reset
  
  
    
    
    
    
    ~135 anchor
    
    ~175 gated
    255 max
  
  the anchor run scores ~135 with NO color sensor — that's what we build first.



135 points, zero color — never trade a reliable anchor for a shaky full run.


This slide connects the whole bootcamp to the scoresheet — and now the mat is physically in the room, point at the real zones while clicking through the tiers. The load-bearing idea: color is risk (lighting, calibration — they just saw the numbers move between surfaces), so we defer every point that needs it. Everything the anchor needs lives color-free: the precise driving and wall verbs this week builds, then the Week-3 team mechanisms — cables, instruments, microphone, avoidance. Fixed notes (+40) arrive Week 4 only after the anchor is locked; randomized notes (+80) are a Week-5 stretch. If a parent asks “why not go for 255 now?”: a reliable 175 beats a heroic 255 you land one time in ten — this is a WRO scoring truth, not a confidence thing.

title: Stop at the Wall
section: Gentle Giant 🐘 — live on the mat
text:
Gentle Giant 🐘 — live on the mat






The challenge


Feel the wall, back off, stop exactly 5 cm away. Closest wins — loop bonus for a second wall in the same run.




Predict your BACKOFF_MM out loud, then prove it
3 attempts, best counts — judge measures nose-to-board
🎸 Roadie preview (fast squads): push a cable brick into the grey zone — library calls only


The competition proves the day’s whole chain: stall detection, calibrated back-off, and (for the bonus) the patrol loop — all on the real mat with the board standing in for the missing border (decision #23). The Roadie preview is decision #22’s mission-preview pattern: the cable task is pure pushing, so the bare chassis can score it — squads discover that the approach angle decides whether the cable stays upright, which is exactly the W3 mechanism conversation, planted early for free. Keep the leaderboard warm but light: most-improved call-out as always. (A practice sim of Gentle Giant lives in the 🏠 appendix.)

title: Stop at the Wall
text:
Today’s gate






You’re done today when…



Your stall + back-off patrol works on two walls in one run
drive_to_wall and wall_square live in your my_lib.py
You can say why the anchor run needs no color sensor





Next: Line King — trace a line at speed with a formula you already own. 🛣️


The gate has three legs: the reactive patrol (stall + calibrated back-off + loop across two walls), the two new library verbs demonstrated from my_lib.py (plus the quick oral check: what does duty_limit protect?), and the scoring understanding (anchor-first). All three are observable at the bench in under 20 minutes with five robots. Optional ½-block if time allows — the plan’s “Good Practices” beat gets its full day at the consolidation session, but a taste here is cheap insurance: pseudocode the patrol in plain words first, comment the threshold with its calibration numbers, and back the working file up before the line day. Then tease D7: same error × gain they built for the gyro, now steering an eye down a line — on the mat’s printed staff lines.

title: Line King
text:
Where we left off



You own
Built on




📈 error × gain
the turn · the straight


🛑 D — damping
brakes before overshoot


👀 the eye, calibrated
echo 0–100 · threshold per surface










One thing up front


Today reads brightness, not color names — colors are Week 4.





Anchor today in what they already own, or it feels like a brand-new mountain (it isn’t — the plan flags D7 as the longest day precisely because the old material was video-paced; live, it’s one formula applied a third time). Recap error × gain from the gyro out loud. Flag the reflection-vs-color point early and firmly — kids will want to “detect the green line”; stop that now, it’s brightness only until Week 4. The day is bang-bang → P on the line, then junction squaring — that pair is the gate (it’s what the anchor run actually uses); the PD upgrade is the stretch for squads that tune P early. If the cohort is still behind on programming, the afternoon flexes to library-consolidation practice instead of pushing PD.

title: Line King
section: Meet the eye: ride the edge
text:
Meet the eye: ride the edge
# Watch it live FIRST — before any steering. Just read the number.
while True:
    print("reflection:", left_eye.reflection())   # 0=black, 100=white
    wait(100)

  
    slide the eye across the edge:
    
  
  
    
    
    white mat
    black line
    
    the edge = threshold 50
    
  
  reflection 88 → error +38 · far onto white, steer hard toward the line




error = reflection − 50 — + on white · − on black · 0 on the edge.



Watch reflection live on a real robot first, over white then over black — read the two numbers aloud, split them, that’s the threshold (re-do on the actual mat; venue lighting shifts it). Then the widget: drag the eye and narrate the single signed number. The key mental model is edge-following, not line-centering — one eye rides the boundary, half on black. Positive error = drifted onto white, negative = drifted onto black. This is deliberately the same shape as the gyro’s error = target − heading. Don’t mention P yet; just establish that one eye yields one clean error.

title: Line King
text:
Break it: only two answers 🔥

Two moves only — hard left or hard right. Predict the path.

while True:
    if left_eye.reflection() < THRESHOLD:
        left.run(LINE_SPEED + TURN_HARD)       # on black → yank right
        right.run(LINE_SPEED - TURN_HARD)
    else:
        left.run(LINE_SPEED - TURN_HARD)       # on white → yank left
        right.run(LINE_SPEED + TURN_HARD)
    wait(10)


Two answers = it can never go straight — it slams left-right forever.



This is bang-bang — the naïve controller. Let them predict, then confirm with the sim two slides on. The diagnosis you want in their words: “it only knows left or right, so it’s always turning.” The bridge to P is exactly the gain quiz from Day 4: what if the correction were proportional to how far off we are, instead of always full-tilt? Keep bang-bang short — it exists only to make P feel like the rescue.

title: Line King
text:
Name it: P — proportional steering 📈
error = left_eye.reflection() - THRESHOLD    # signed: how far off the edge?
left.run(LINE_SPEED + KP * error)            # far → hard steer,
right.run(LINE_SPEED - KP * error)           # near → gentle


error × gain again — tune KP: too small = lazy, too big = the wobble is back.



The “aha” is that they’ve written this line before — twice. It’s literally the D4 self-healing straight with a new error source: swap hub.imu.heading() for the eye’s reflection and the two run lines are identical. Same error × gain, different sensor feeding it. Two failure modes to name so they recognize them on the bench: sluggish (KP too low) drifts wide on curves; twitchy (KP too high) reintroduces the wobble. Start small and adjust. This alone wins “Line King” on gentle lines — but push the speed up on a curve and it starts to overshoot, which is the doorway to the PD stretch. If a student raises the “use motor.dc()” advice here: same answer as the D4 proportional-turn coach note — run() keeps “speed” meaning speed, so KP * error stays a correction in deg/s they can reason about.

bang-bang ping-pongs · P overshoots · PD holds. Drag KD → 0 — D is the only difference.



Run bang-bang first (big zigzag), then P at speed 200 (fine on the straight, overshoots into the S), then PD (clean). The money move is live: with PD selected, drag KD to 0 — the green curve starts ringing exactly like P; nudge it back up and it settles. That’s the same demonstration as Day 5’s kd slider on the gyro, now on the line — same D, same job, new sensor. Crank the speed slider to show P’s overshoot grows with speed while PD holds. Sim is illustrative; on the real robot they tune the P gain first, and only squads chasing the stretch touch kd.

title: Line King
section: Name it: follow_line joins the library 📦
text:
Name it: follow_line joins the library 📦
def follow_line(distance_mm, speed=250, kd=0):
    """P follow on the LEFT eye — kd > 0 is the PD stretch."""
    finish = left.angle() + distance_mm * DEG_PER_MM
    last_error = 0
    while left.angle() < finish:
        error = left_eye.reflection() - THRESHOLD
        steer = KP_LINE * error + kd * (error - last_error)
        left.run(speed + steer)
        right.run(speed - steer)
        last_error = error
        wait(10)
    left.stop(); right.stop()


The P loop, bottled — a finish line in degrees makes it a segment. kd is the stretch knob.



Same promotion ritual as every day this week: the loose P loop gets a name and moves into my_lib.py (the gain becomes KP_LINE — the library’s constants block already owns KP for the gyro). Map every part back to what they own: error = P on the eye, the finish line = drive_straight’s DEG_PER_MM trick (follow for a distance — that’s how missions call it), kd * (error - last_error) = the D from the library-day gyro turn, with last_error the one-line memory that makes D possible. The gate is the P version — follow_line(600) with kd=0; the stretch is sliding kd up until the fast curve stops overshooting, which is what wins Speed Demon. State the I-policy plainly and move on: integral fixes a steady offset over a long run; our segments are short, so I is a demo they can watch but never something the gate depends on.

title: Line King
text:
The two-eye job: square up 📐
The second eye’s moment: you hit a junction crooked — sit square before the next move.
def square_on_line(speed=160):
    """Stop each wheel as ITS OWN eye reaches the line."""
    left_done = right_done = False
    left.run(speed); right.run(speed)
    while not (left_done and right_done):
        if not left_done and left_eye.reflection() < THRESHOLD:
            left.hold(); left_done = True      # park left wheel
        if not right_done and right_eye.reflection() < THRESHOLD:
            right.hold(); right_done = True    # park right wheel
        wait(5)


Each eye stops its own wheel — the robot pivots square. That’s why it carries two.



Answer the port question head-on: two color sensors fill two of the six hub ports (B and F), and following needs only one — so the second eye earns its seat here, squaring. On the real mat this kills accumulated drift in a fraction of a second, gold going into the chained runs of Week 3. (The same right eye later reads note colors in Week 4 — but never steers.)

title: Line King
text:
Watch it snap square 📐

  
    ▶ creep onto the junction
  
  
    
    junction
    
      
      
      
    
    arrives crooked — each eye stops its own wheel
  




Leading wheel parks first · trailing wheel swings square — drift gone. 🎯



Run it live: the bot arrives crooked, one wheel stops on the line first, the other pivots the body until its eye lands too — square. This is exactly square_on_line() from the previous slide. On the mat it erases accumulated drift in a fraction of a second, right before the next move — cheap, automatic insurance the chained runs of Week 3 lean on.

title: Line King
text:
Line King 👑 / Speed Demon 🏁






The challenge


Line King — hold the curved line for 30 s: accuracy + speed. Speed Demon (fast squads) — fastest run that never leaves the line.





The winning recipe

Calibrate on the mat you run on
Tune KP_LINE, then chase speed
Square at the junction to reset
(stretch) kd kills the curve overshoot


Where squads lose it

Threshold from yesterday’s surface 😬
KP so high the straight wobbles
Skipping the square → drift compounds



Line King rewards a clean, honest tune over raw speed; Speed Demon is the stretch for squads whose P was solid early and who took the kd upgrade. Coach’s eye is on the tuning conversation, not the trophy: can they explain what the error means, what KP_LINE did, and why the threshold had to be re-measured on this mat? That explanation IS the gate (kd talk is stretch credit, not a requirement). Keep the taped junction handy so squaring gets used under pressure, not just demoed.

title: Line King
text:
Today’s gate






You’re done today when…



You can build, tune, and explain a P line-follower
It follows a curve and squares on a junction reliably
follow_line + square_on_line live in your my_lib.py — (stretch: the kd upgrade)





Next: five files become one clean my_lib.py — a gauntlet only library calls can beat. 📦


The gate is the plan’s D7 outcome: build, tune, and explain a P follower + reliable junction squaring — what the anchor run actually uses; PD is explicitly the stretch, never the gate. A student who can narrate why each term is there owns the concept and can debug it in Week 3 when the follower serves the run. Two eyes, one job each: one follows, both square. Close by pointing forward: next is the consolidation day — one module, one constants block, the challenge gauntlet — then missions become lists; the point-scoring mechanisms arrive with the Week-3 team builds, feeding the anchor-first 255 map they read yesterday.

title: The Setlist
text:
Where we left off







You have
What it does




📦 one my_lib.py
every verb in one module — one constants block tunes them all


🚗 moving verbs
drive_straight · turn_to — watched by your PD loop


🧱 wall verbs
drive_to_wall · wall_square — the motor as a touch sensor


👀 line verbs
follow_line · square_on_line — the mat as a map




🤔 The gauntlet proved every verb works alone. A competition run is verbs in a row — and the row keeps changing. Today: how pros hold a plan that has to change in seconds.

⏱️ Time: 10 minutes

Recap by asking: name the verbs, who tuned what, which constant fixes which move. The framing sentence for the day: your library is the vocabulary; today you get the sentence. Foreshadow the pain honestly — the judge at the end of today will call a mission and then change it, and squads whose plan is a pasted block will lose minutes where a list squad loses seconds. That’s the break-it, and it’s also exactly what the Surprise Rule does at a real WRO table.

title: The Setlist
text:
Unplugged: the pigeonholes 📮

Six numbered boxes on the bench, a pile of colored bricks. “Red into box 0. Blue into box 2. What’s in box 1? Swap 0 and 3. Add a green one at the end — which box did it land in?”



You just used every list skill: a numbered row of slots, filled in order, counting from 0 — read a slot by its number (index), walk the row front to back (iterate), grow it at the end (append). Python calls this row a list. No laptop needed — you already think this way.



Run it physically before any code — 5 minutes, real boxes (or taped squares) and real bricks, quick-fire calls. The two stumbles to engineer on purpose: (1) “box 1” when they expect counting from 1 — let someone grab the wrong brick, then name the rule: robots count from 0; (2) append lands at the end, position = old length. Keep the boxes on the bench all day — every list question later gets answered by pointing at them. This is WRO-Learn’s arrays intro compressed into a game.

title: The Setlist
text:
Break it: the pasted plan 🔥

The judge calls: cross the stage · face the band · square on the wall. You paste it as one block. Then: “Actually — wall square FIRST.” How long does the edit take?

# mission_friday.py — one pasted block 😬
drive_straight(400)          # cross the stage
turn_to(90)           # face the band
drive_to_wall()
wall_square()         # reset at the wall
# judge: "wall square FIRST, then cross"…
# select lines… which? cut… paste… 🐛


The plan is trapped in the code. Reordering means surgery — select, cut, paste, and pray you moved all the lines of each step. Under a 5-minute clock, surgery loses. The plan needs to be a thing you can shuffle — like cards in a deck.



Let a squad actually attempt the live edit under a visible countdown — the fumble is the lesson. Diagnose together: the steps are clear in their heads (“cross”, “face”, “square”) but the code has no such boundaries; a step is 1–3 loose lines with nothing holding them together. Two missing ideas, teased in order: give each step a name (they own def — this is a two-minute step), then put the names in a row that can be shuffled — the pigeonholes from the warm-up. Don’t show the fix yet.

title: The Setlist
text:
Lists in sixty seconds
setlist = ["intro", "solo", "finale"]   # a numbered row of slots
setlist[0]                # "intro" — count from 0, like the boxes!
setlist.append("encore")  # grows at the end → slot 3
setlist[1] = "duet"       # swap what a slot holds
for song in setlist:      # visit every slot, front to back
    print(song)


Index · append · iterate — the three moves from the pigeonhole game, now in Python. A list holds anything: strings, numbers… and (next slide) something sneakier. 😏



Type it live in the REPL, mirroring each line back to the physical boxes on the bench (“slot 0… that box”). Off-by-one and negative index (setlist[-1] = last box) are fun 30-second asides if the room is quick. Don’t linger — the payoff slide is next, and lists only get interesting once functions go inside them.

title: The Setlist
section: Steps are just functions — hold the name, don’t play it
text:
Steps are just functions — hold the name, don’t play it
def cross_stage():
    drive_straight(400)

def face_band():
    turn_to(90)

def reset_at_wall():
    drive_to_wall(); wall_square()

mission = [reset_at_wall, cross_stage, face_band]
#          ^ no parentheses — cards in a deck, not moves happening


cross_stage() plays the move. cross_stage — no parentheses — is the recipe card itself, a thing you can hold, hand around… and drop into a list. A list of names is a setlist: the whole show, and it hasn’t started yet.



This is the day’s one genuinely new idea — a function is a value. Land it with the recipe-card metaphor from the first def day: the card vs cooking the dish. Common bug to demo on purpose: write mission = [cross_stage(), face_band()] — the robot drives while the list is being built (the moves happen at line 10!) and the list ends up holding three Nones. Let them see that, then delete the parentheses. Note the judge’s reorder is now already solved on line 10 — reset_at_wall simply moved to slot 0.

title: The Setlist
text:
The runner — the whole thing
def run_mission(steps):
    for step in steps:   # take the next card…
        step()           # …now play it
run_mission(mission)


Three lines, and they never change again. Reorder the show → edit the list. Insert a step → append or slot it in. The judge changes the mission; you change data, not code. That’s the split the pros live by.



Read it aloud in pigeonhole language: for each box front to back, take the card out, play it. The deep point — worth saying explicitly, once: the runner is control and the mission is data, and everything that changes lives in the data. This exact runner drives the anchor run in Week 3; per-step timeouts and a transition log are its stretch upgrade (the exemplar’s run_mission_logged) — offer to fast squads at the gate, never gate on it. A non-blocking state machine is continuation-season material; RoboMission runs are sequential, blocking is right.

title: The Setlist
text:
Feel it: shuffle the setlist 🔀

  
    ▶ run_mission(mission)
    🔀 judge: wall square FIRST
    ➕ append(face_crowd)
  
  
    
      mission = [
      
      ]
    
    
      
      wall
      
      start
      
      
        
        
        
      
      
    
  




The judge’s reorder is one edit to the list — watch slot 0 change hands, then run the same three-line runner. Append a step: a new slot appears at the end, exactly like the pigeonholes. The plan is data now.



Ritual: run the original show first so they see list-highlighting track the robot (the ▶ walks down the slots as the robot moves — index made visible). Then hit the judge button without running: only the list changes, code untouched — say it out loud. Run again: wall square happens first. Append, run a third time. Connect each button to the pigeonhole verbs: swap = re-slotting cards, append = new box at the end. Then send them to the real mat to do the identical thing with their own verbs — the sim is the storyboard, the mat is the exam.

title: The Setlist
text:
Setlist 🎵






The challenge


The judge calls a 3-step mini-mission — you have 5 minutes to encode it as a mission list over your own library and run it. Score = steps completed; tiebreak = time.




Hear the mission → say it as verbs (“that’s a drive_straight, a turn_to, a wall_square”) → wrap, list, run.
The judge will change the order on round two. 😈 Your surgery-free edit is the whole advantage.
Fast squads: swap in run_mission_logged — per-step stopwatch + a printed transition log.


Score decomposition, not typing speed: the squads to praise are the ones who translate the judge’s sentence into library verbs before touching a keyboard. Round two’s reorder is the day’s thesis under pressure — a list squad edits one line; watch for anyone still cutting and pasting blocks and coach them on the spot. The logged runner is the exemplar’s stretch (timeouts + transition log) — offer it, never gate it. Coach’s private scorecard today: list fluency, decomposition into verbs, debugging a sequence (which step failed? the log says).

title: The Setlist
text:
Today’s gate






You’re done today when…


You can read and build a list (index from 0, iterate, append), your steps are named functions over your own library, and the three-line runner walks a judge-called 3-step mission — with a reorder costing you one edit, not surgery.




Next: Draft Day — teams form, each team picks its competition robot… and a surprise you’ve spent two weeks earning. 🎁


The gate is the plan’s D9 outcome verbatim. This is the last solo-robot day — tomorrow’s session opens with the Mini Mission finale and the draft, so make sure every student’s library + runner are saved and named (they carry into the team merge, which is exactly what motivates the config lesson there). Keep the teaser vague on purpose: the “surprise” is the DriveBase reveal, and it lands best unspoiled.

title: The Earned Shortcut
text:
Where we left off







You own
What it does




📦 my_lib.py
drive_straight · turn_to · drive_to_wall · wall_square · follow_line · square_on_line — one module, one constants block


🎵 the runner
a mission is a list; three lines walk it


🏁 gauntlet + setlist
every verb proven alone and in sequence, under a judge’s clock




🤔 Everything above was built by one student, for one robot. Today five students become two teams — and each team keeps just one robot. What happens to four robots’ worth of carefully tuned code?

⏱️ Time: 10 minutes

Recap fast — the room already owns all of it; the table is there to make the inventory visible because today it gets audited. The warm-up question is the day’s whole arc: the merge (code from several authors onto one robot) is the break-it that motivates RobotConfig, and the reveal + draft close the two-week chapter. Structure of the day: Mini Mission finale runs in the competition window as usual; the draft itself lands after the gate. Keep the DriveBase surprise unspoiled until Act C.

title: The Earned Shortcut
text:
Break it: the merge 🔥

Your team just picked its robot — and it’s not yours. Your my_lib.py thinks the wheels are 88 mm; the chosen robot’s are 87. Your teammate’s turn gain is tuned hotter. A motor sits on a different port. How many edits until your mission runs on this robot?


  
    sara/my_lib.py
    WHEEL_MM = 88
    KP = 4
    left = Motor(Port.A)
  
  
    omar/my_lib.py
    WHEEL_MM = 87
    KP = 6
    left = Motor(Port.B)
  
  
    lena/my_lib.py
    WHEEL_MM = 90 😱
    KP = 3.5
    left = Motor(Port.A)
  
  🤖ONE robot



Every file was tuned for a robot that just became a parts donor. The facts — wheels, ports, gains, speeds — are scattered across files and functions: a robot swap means twenty hunted edits, and missing one means the robot lies quietly. The facts need one home.



Make it concrete with the team’s actual files if the draft order allows — three real constants blocks on screen beats the mock-up. Sharpen the sting: it’s not just today’s merge. The donor pool means a swap can happen again mid-season (a cracked chassis at Week 4 pace), and next time there’s no afternoon to spare. Elicit the shape of the fix before naming it — they’ve done this twice: constants block (square day), library module (consolidation day). Same instinct, one level up: promote the constants to a single shared object the whole team’s code reads.

title: The Earned Shortcut
section: Fix it: RobotConfig — the robot in one place
text:
Fix it: RobotConfig — the robot in one place
@dataclass
class RobotConfig:
    wheel_diameter: int = 88    # mm — MEASURE the team robot
    axle_track: int = 145       # mm — wheel center to center
    left_port: Port = Port.A    # re-port a motor = one edit
    right_port: Port = Port.E
    turn_gain: float = 4.0      # the tuned knobs live here too
    straight_speed: int = 300   # mm/s
    backoff_mm: int = 50        # the measured back-off

CFG = RobotConfig()             # the ONE copy everyone reads


A @dataclass is a labeled box for facts — every physical truth about the robot, in one file (robot_config.py), with a name on each line. Nothing else in the team’s code is allowed to know a number about the robot; it has to ask CFG.



@dataclass is presented as “a class whose whole job is holding named facts” — no OOP lecture; it’s a form they fill in, and the decorator writes the boring plumbing. (Coach note: Pybricks MicroPython has no dataclasses module — the exemplar robot_config.py ships a 3-line try/except shim so the same syntax runs on the hub; hand it out rather than typing it.) Walk the categories: geometry, ports, tuned knobs — point out these are exactly the numbers that differed across the three files on the last slide. The full gold-reference config (all 6 ports, line thresholds, stall limits) is in the root exemplar; teams copy it and edit their measured values.

title: The Earned Shortcut
section: Every verb now asks the config
text:
Every verb now asks the config
# my_lib.py — the verbs READ the config, they don't know numbers
left  = Motor(CFG.left_port, Direction.COUNTERCLOCKWISE)
right = Motor(CFG.right_port)

DEG_PER_MM = 360 / (3.14 * CFG.wheel_diameter)

def turn_to(target, gain=None, kd=None):
    gain = gain or CFG.turn_gain
    kd = kd or CFG.turn_kd
    # …the same PD loop as ever…


New robot? Measure it, edit robot_config.py, done — the library and every mission file never change. The donor pool just became a one-line insurance policy. 🛡️



The team exercise that proves it (from the exemplar): run the merged code with teammate A’s old constants on the chosen robot — watch the square miss; then fix ONLY robot_config.py and rerun — the square closes. One file changed, zero functions touched. That felt difference is the lesson. Also flag the discipline cost honestly: from today, hard-coding a number inside a function is a team foul — a merge conflict waiting to happen. The constants block habit from the library days is what made this promotion cheap.

title: The Earned Shortcut
text:
You built a robot driver 🏆


You wrote, these two weeks

turn_to(target) — a PD turn that watches itself, brakes before it arrives, and lands true
drive_straight(distance) — a self-healing straight with a finish line in degrees


Every WRO robot needs

turns that are true, not “about right”
straights that survive bumps, seams, and battery moods




Pybricks ships both moves — polished, tuned, tested — inside one tool: DriveBase. It runs a professional P·I·D on every move. You’ll understand every letter it does… because you spent two weeks building all three.



This is the payoff the whole fortnight has been walking toward — let it feel like a gift, not a rug-pull. The frame that keeps their work dignified: professionals don’t hand-roll what a trusted library ships, but they can only trust it because they know what’s inside — and this room now does. If anyone asks “why didn’t we start here?”: because then DriveBase would be magic, and magic can’t be debugged at a competition table. Nothing they built was wasted — the next slides show exactly which verbs survive.

title: The Earned Shortcut
text:
Measure your robot first

DriveBase asks for two numbers: wheel diameter and axle track (tire-center to tire-center). Why does it need the second one?
Hint: you already used it once — on Day 3…



It’s your turn-circle! You ran that math by hand once to get 148 — DriveBase runs your derivation for any angle. The shortcut is math you already own.



Both numbers are measured, not copied: roll the wheel 5 turns, divide by 5 and π for diameter (they’ll land near 88); the track they measured on Day 3 for the 148 derivation (≈145 mm on our build; the stock ADB is ≈114) — have each team re-measure its chosen robot now, since that’s the number going into its config. Wrong track → every turn systematically over- or under-shoots: that’s the afternoon’s tuning conversation (drive.settings()). If asked why the track matters: a spin-in-place drives the wheels around a circle as wide as the axle track — wider track = more wheel-degrees per robot-degree.

title: The Earned Shortcut
section: Build your DriveBase — from the config
text:
Build your DriveBase — from the config
from pybricks.robotics import DriveBase   # now earned

drive = DriveBase(left, right,
                  CFG.wheel_diameter, CFG.axle_track)
drive.use_gyro(True)  # your gyro + full PID, switched on
drive.settings(straight_speed=CFG.straight_speed,
               turn_rate=150)

DriveBase speaks mm and robot-degrees — drive.straight(300), drive.turn(90) — and with use_gyro(True) every move is watched and PID-corrected, exactly like yours were. And it reads its facts from CFG, so it survives a robot swap like everything else.


Two acts of the day snap together here: the reveal is wired straight into the config, so the shortcut is born team-proof. drive.settings() is the pro version of their tuned constants — speeds and accelerations, adjustable per robot, stored in the config. Also worth naming: drive.stalled() now replaces the per-motor stall trick from the wall day inside DriveBase moves, and drive.distance() is a free odometer. Don’t tour the whole API — the duel is next; let the tool prove itself.

title: The Earned Shortcut
section: The duel: your verb vs the pro’s 🥊
text:
The duel: your verb vs the pro’s 🥊

  
    ▶ drive_straight(1000) vs drive.straight(1000)
    😈 nudge them BOTH
  
  
    
    
    YOURS — drive_straight
    PRO — drive.straight
    
    
    1 m 🎯
    
    
    
      
      
      
    
    
      
      
      
    
  
  yours landed – · pro landed –




Nudge them both: both heal — same three letters inside. The pro edges ahead on polish (smooth speed ramps, factory-tuned gains, years of testing), not on magic. You didn’t get replaced — you hired a chauffeur for the part you already mastered.



Run the duel on the real robots too — their drive_straight next to drive.straight under a nudge and a battery swap; the sim just makes the point projectable. Expected honest outcome: both survive, DriveBase is a bit crisper on arrival (its speed ramps are S-curves, its gains factory-tuned per motor model). The verdict to speak: hand-rolled took two weeks to build and would take another season to polish; the pro one is free and made of math they own. That’s why the reveal waited — a shortcut is only a shortcut if you know the long way.

title: The Earned Shortcut
text:
What retires, what stays yours


Retires today 🪦

drive_straight(d) → drive.straight(d)
turn_to(a) → drive.turn(…) (gyro-watched)
hand-tuned KP/kd → drive.settings()


Stays in my_lib.py 💪

drive_to_wall() · wall_square() — the feel verbs
follow_line() · square_on_line() — the eyes verbs
every mechanism verb Week 3 will add




DriveBase replaces exactly two internals — the moving. It has no idea how to feel a wall, ride a line’s edge, or work a mechanism. The library keeps every verb the pro tool doesn’t have — which is most of them. Missions from here on: DriveBase drives, your library does everything else.



This boundary slide is the anti-demoralizer — put numbers on it: two verbs retire, four survive today, and every mechanism verb from Week 3 joins the surviving side. Mechanically the retirement is gentle: inside my_lib, drive_straight/turn_to can simply become one-line wrappers over drive.straight/drive.turn, so every mission list and runner from the setlist day works unchanged — nothing downstream even notices. (That’s the payoff of naming moves: swap the engine, keep the verbs.) drive.stalled() similarly upgrades the inside of drive_to_wall without changing its name.

title: The Earned Shortcut
text:
Mini Mission 🎤






The challenge


A 3-task run — drive, turn, push an object home — in 60 seconds. Everything from two weeks, on one clock: integration, time management, nerve.




Plan it as a mission list over your verbs — you have a runner for exactly this.
Budget the clock: a clean 2-task run beats a chaotic 3-task attempt. (Sound familiar? It’s the anchor-first rule, in miniature.)


Template is Competition Ready Lesson 8 “Mission Ready.” This is the last solo competition and the scorecard’s final data point — watch integration and composure, not just score. Call out anyone who deliberately drops to two tasks to bank clean points: that’s competition maturity, praise it loudly — it’s the exact instinct the anchor-first strategy needs from them in Week 4. After scores are recorded, close the leaderboard era warmly: from tomorrow the competition is team vs team.

title: The Earned Shortcut
text:
The draft 🤝

Teams are formed on who works well together and what each team needs — complementary skills beat matching skills.
Each team reviews its members’ robots and selects one as its competition robot.
The unselected robots become the parts donor pool — your insurance, one config edit away. 🛡️
From tomorrow: team vs team, every day — double the mat time, twice the fun.



Your robot may retire to the donor pool — your code and your skills don’t. Everything merges onto the team robot through robot_config.py, and both of the team’s cross-trained roles need every verb you built. The draft picks a chassis, not a coder.



Say the framing line exactly as planned and nothing more: “I’m teaming you based on who works well together and what each team needs” — never rank, never “you scored lowest.” The pairings are already decided from the private scorecard + library benchmarks; announce, don’t debate. Give the selection its dignity: each team physically inspects its candidate robots (the four quality checks from Day 1 make a nice ritual return) before choosing. Handle the sting of an unselected robot with the card’s line — the donor pool is genuinely valuable, not a consolation. Set up team build areas after the announcement.

title: The Earned Shortcut
text:
Today’s gate






You’re done today when…


Your library passes its benchmarks one last time solo (drive_straight ±2 cm · turn_to ±2° · wall and line verbs demonstrated · the runner walks a called mission) — and your team leaves with one chosen robot, a donor pool, and a single robot_config.py that drives it with the merged code.




Next week: your robot grows hands — the mechanisms that start turning driving skill into scored points, and the runner starts chaining them into the anchor run. 🔌🎤


The student-facing end-of-week-2 gate: individual benchmarks (they’re part of the draft evidence) plus the team artifacts — chosen robot, donor pool, one working config. The coach’s own checklist for today (gold-reference validation against the same gate, dimensions/weight/wireless-off, CAD capture in BrickLink Studio) runs off-stage and isn’t student material. Teaser lands on Week 3: mechanisms first — cables and microphone, instruments and avoidance — then the mission runner chains the ~135 anchor.

title: Build Day
text:
Hands up! ✋
Stand up or hands up — who has…

🧱 …built something out of LEGO?
💻 …written a line of code?
🤖 …watched a robot competition — live?



By the end of today you’ll have built a real competition robot with your own hands — and switched it on. The code starts next session.

title: Build Day
text:
Robots that think for themselves



Teams build and program a robot that solves missions all on its own.
Every year: a brand-new mission — nobody starts with last year’s answers.
Win here → national final → the world final.



Our category
RoboMission · Elementary — the robot drives a mission on a printed mat, on a real competition table.

title: Build Day
text:
Watch a real run


WRO 2026 Elementary — the Robot Rockstars field. In five weeks, this is your table. (watch on YouTube)


YouTube refuses to play when the rendered HTML is opened straight from disk (file:// → “Error 153”). Present via quarto preview or any local HTTP server and it plays fine; the caption link is the in-room fallback.

title: Build Day
text:
Quick quiz 🚨

Your robot is mid-run — and it’s heading straight off the table. What can you do?
Grab it? Shout “STOP”? Grab a joystick?



Nothing. You press one start button — after that, the robot is completely alone. Everything it does comes from the code you wrote.




Each run: at most 2 minutes · robot starts inside a 25 × 25 × 25 cm box.

title: Build Day
text:
This year’s stage: Robot Rockstars 🎸
The robot sets up a concert — all by itself:

🥁 Set up the instruments on stage
🔌 Plug in the cables
🎤 Deliver the microphone to the singer
🔊 Don’t bump the speakers — careful driving counts
🎵 Crack the color notes — new positions every round, so the robot must look and decide


Vote: which one sounds hardest? 🗳️

title: Build Day
section: One more twist — the Surprise Rule 🎁
text:
One more twist — the Surprise Rule 🎁

On competition morning, judges may reveal a surprise — a new task or a changed rule. Teams reprogram on the spot.

That’s why we understand our code — never memorize it. Every team member can explain the whole robot. No “that’s my partner’s part.”

title: Build Day
text:
Five weeks, five new powers



Week
Your robot learns to…




1
Build, drive straight, and sense


2
Score its first missions · teams of 2 form


3
Chain everything into one big run


4
Do it every single time


5
Sense color — and compete




A run that works every time beats a fancy one that sometimes doesn’t. You leave with real Python, a robot you built, and a certificate.

title: Build Day
text:
Bench rules

🔋 USB-C charging — hubs charge by cable; know where yours lives.
🗂️ Sort your parts into the tray — a lost axle stops a build.
🤖 One student, one robot — today everyone builds their own.
🔌 No internet connection — wireless is off at competition, so we practice that way from day one.







Bench tour


Find the charging station, the parts bins, the mat, and the return tray — before your hands touch a brick.

title: Build Day
text:
The build — 4 modules, one robot

The base splits into 4 sub-assemblies — build them one at a time, then snap them together.
2 large motors drive the wheels · 2 medium motors run tools · 2 color sensors see the mat.
Follow the official LEGO instructions, page by page — no freestyling on day one.
Plan for 2–3 hours. That’s normal — this is a real competition chassis, not a toy set.







The instructions


LEGO Education — Assembling an Advanced Driving Base — building-instruction booklets for every sub-assembly.




Showpiece hook: you may show Droid Bot IV as a first-day “wow” — it’s a hook only; we clone the Advanced Driving Base, not the droid. The full coach resource is the parent unit: https://education.lego.com/en-us/lessons/prime-competition-ready/ (Training Camps 1–3, My Code Our Program, Time for an Upgrade). Coach builds this same base as the gold reference.

title: Build Day
text:
Pace yourself — module checkpoints 🏁



Checkpoint
You should have…




☑ 1
Drive module — both large motors mounted


☑ 2
Wheels on — it rolls when you push it


☑ 3
Tool motors + sensors attached


☑ 4
Hub seated, all cables clicked in









Lightning Builder ⚡


A bonus for the first finishers whose robot passes the quality checks — a carrot, not a cutoff. If you don’t finish today, you finish in tomorrow’s warm-up. No penalty.

title: Build Day
text:
What makes a base GREAT?
Before you call it done, run the four checks:

🔌 Click check — every cable pushed in until it clicks, routed away from the wheels.
🪞 Twin check — left side mirrors right. Compare with the booklet, not your memory.
🫨 Shake check — lift it, shake gently: nothing rattles, nothing drops.
🛞 Roll check — push it across the mat: rolls straight, nothing drags.



A base that passes all four beats a fancy one that loses a wheel mid-run.

title: Build Day
text:
6 ports, 6 jobs






The hub has only 6 ports — and we use every one


A left drive · E right drive · C/D tool motors · B/F color sensors. No port left over — so no distance sensor. Later, you’ll learn a cooler trick: the motors themselves can feel a wall.




Plug each cable into its exact port from the start — the code we write all season expects this map.

title: Build Day
text:
Install Pybricks first 🧑‍💻
Before the hub can run our Python, it needs new firmware — Pybricks.






The editor


code.pybricks.com — the browser code editor we use all season. No install, no account. Bookmark it.




Open code.pybricks.com.
Click the ⚙️ gear icon → Install Pybricks Firmware.
Plug the hub in by USB-C when asked, and wait for the progress bar to finish.

title: Build Day
text:
Open the file panel
Click the file icon (top-left) to open the file panel — this is where every program you write all season will live.

title: Build Day
text:
Create your first file
Click +, choose program, name it main, pick your hub, then Create — Pybricks drops in a starter template.



One hub, one main.py — the file the coach’s D1 program runs from today, and the file you write into starting next session.

title: Build Day
text:
Bring it to life

Charge check — plug in USB-C; the battery light tells the truth.
Press the center button — hold until the light comes on.
Watch the status light — that blink is the hub saying “I’m alive, give me a program.”
Connect by cable to the laptop — hear it beep. 🔊



That beep is a program — a few lines of Python that the coach sent to your robot. Next session, you write it.



Coach prep: hubs must be flashed with Pybricks firmware (code.pybricks.com) before or during D1 — budget time for it. The beep “proof of life” is d01_power_on.py from the repo root, run over the USB cable, one hub at a time. Let each student press the button themselves — the moment matters more than the minutes it costs.

title: Build Day

You’re done today when…


Your base passes the four checks, every cable is in the right port, and your hub powers on and beeps.




Next session — Make It Move: your first race, your first program, and what speed really is — measured on your own robot.
