title: Drive a Square
text:
Where we left off



Yesterday
The trick




📏 Distance
count degrees — 1 turn = 27.6 cm


🧭 Blindfolded
no checking → it drifts




🤔 It knows how far its wheels spun. But which way is it pointing?

⏱️ Time: 10 minutes

title: Drive a Square
text:
Setup — same as yesterday
from pybricks.hubs import PrimeHub
from pybricks.pupdevices import Motor
from pybricks.parameters import Port, Direction
from pybricks.tools import wait

hub = PrimeHub()
left = Motor(Port.A, Direction.COUNTERCLOCKWISE)
right = Motor(Port.E)

Everyone types this once at the top and keeps it for the whole day. Raw motors only, on purpose — through D9 the students build the whole driver and library by hand; the polished shortcut (DriveBase) waits until the D10 reveal (decision #20), a reward earned piece by piece.

title: Drive a Square
text:
One corner = how many degrees? 🤔

A side is easy: 30 cm → 391°. But when the robot spins in place, how far does each wheel roll?
Watch the wheels from above — what shape do they draw?


Sketch it. Write down a wheel-degrees guess. 👀


Pure prediction — do NOT derive or reveal anything here. Collect shape guesses (a line? a spiral? a circle?) and a wheel-degrees number from every squad on the board; wrong guesses make the next slide land harder. The derivation happens after the demo’s traces give the shape away. Do NOT mention slip or scrub anywhere in this act — the math is honest, and the square is about to betray it anyway. That betrayal lands harder if they trust the number when it arrives.

title: Drive a Square
text:
The wheels answer: the turn-circle 🛞

  
    
    
    
    
      
      
      
      
      
    
    
  
  
    
      robot turned: 0°
      tire rolled: 0.0 cm → 0°
    
    
      rope around the circle = 3.14 × 14.5 ≈ 45.5 cm
      one corner = rope ÷ 4 ≈ 11.4 cm of rolling
      × 13.04 (yesterday's translator) → TURN_ANGLE ≈ 148°
    
    
      ▶ Turn 90°
      ▶ Turn 180°
      ↺ reset
    
    
      tire gap: 14.5 cm
      
    
  




That gap has a name: the axle track — the wheel-to-wheel distance, ≈ 14.5 cm on our base. Wider robot → longer rope → bigger corner. Measure your own — nobody copies 148.



This is the reveal for the last slide’s question — click BEFORE explaining. Hit Turn 90° with their guesses on the board: both tires drag a quarter of the dashed circle, the shape question answers itself on screen, and the ¼ rope ≈ 11.4 cm label lands right on the arc they just watched. Now the three formula lines, one fragment at a time, each reading its number off the picture: rope around the circle (3.14 × gap) — say it as yesterday’s move: “rope around the rim gave you 27.6; same trick, bigger circle, drawn on the mat” — then a quarter of it (the labeled arc), × 13.04 → 148, next to their guesses. Don’t re-teach the translator — 13.04 is yesterday’s number; a quick “who remembers what 13.04 does?” is enough. Click three more times: a full robot-turn = the whole circle’s rope. Save the Turn 180° button for the about-face slide two slides ahead — it shows ½ rope live and confirms ≈297. Then the payoff: drag the tire gap slider wider — every line updates live and TURN_ANGLE grows. Wider robot = more rolling per corner — that’s why every squad measures their own gap (≈145 mm on our build; the stock ADB is ≈114 mm). Teal trace = right tire, coral = left, same colors as the code comments.

title: Drive a Square
text:
Unroll the formula 🧵
Your 148 is right. But every number is one of yesterday’s ropes — unroll them, and watch π vanish:

  TURN_ANGLE = 3.14 × 14.5 ÷ 4  × 13.04
             = 3.14 × 14.5 ÷ 4  × 360 ÷ (3.14 × 8.8)
             = 3.14 × 14.5 × 90 ÷ (3.14 × 8.8)
             = 90 × 14.5 ÷ 8.8 ≈ 148°



TURN_ANGLE = turn × gap ÷ wheel. The two 3.14s cancel. Keep gap and wheel in your pocket… 😏



Act 3 of the turn-circle story: question → wheels answer → unroll the answer. Elicit the substitution, don’t re-teach it — both ropes are Day-2 property: “what IS 13.04?” (360 ÷ 27.6) and “what was 27.6?” (3.14 × 8.8, the wheel’s rope). The only NEW thing on this slide is the cancellation: same 3.14 on top and bottom → cancels; 360 ÷ 4 → 90. What’s left is 90 × gap ÷ wheel: sanity-check on the calculator, 90 × 14.5 ÷ 8.8 = 148.3 ≈ their 148. The pocket line is a deliberate plant: gap and wheel diameter are exactly the two numbers DriveBase asks for on Day 5 — when that deck asks “why does it need the axle track?”, call back to this cancellation. Land at least the final gap ÷ wheel line with everyone; if a squad is drowning in the algebra, the demo intuition carries the load — skim the middle steps and move on.

title: Drive a Square
section: Prove you own it: the about-face 🔄
text:
Prove you own it: the about-face 🔄

Judge wants a full 180° about-face. In turn × gap ÷ wheel — which number changes?
Say it first, then calculate.



  corner:     90 × 14.5 ÷ 8.8 ≈ 148°
  about-face: 180 × 14.5 ÷ 8.8 ≈ 297°




Only the turn changed — half the rope, so ≈ double. Gap and wheel = your robot’s fingerprint. The turn = your order.



This is the payoff of the unroll: the formula generalizes. Elicit before revealing — most squads will say “the 90 becomes 180”; make them commit, then show the line. If anyone doubts it, flip back two slides and hit Turn 180° on the turn-circle demo: the label shows ½ rope ≈ 22.8 cm and the tire counter lands on ≈297 — the picture and the formula agree. Fast squads: ask for the octagon corner (45° → 45 × 14.5 ÷ 8.8 ≈ 74°) — they’ll need it after the loop slide. Every squad substitutes their own measured gap, not 14.5.

title: Drive a Square
text:
The square — by hand
SIDE_ANGLE = 391    # 30 cm × 13.04 — yesterday's translator
TURN_ANGLE = 148    # YOUR number — ¼ turn-circle, translated. Honest math!
SPEED = 400         # deg/s

left.run_angle(SPEED, SIDE_ANGLE, wait=False)    # side 1
right.run_angle(SPEED, SIDE_ANGLE)
left.run_angle(SPEED, TURN_ANGLE, wait=False)    # corner 1 — wheels roll opposite ways…
right.run_angle(SPEED, -TURN_ANGLE)              # …so the robot spins in place

# your turn: copy-paste until you have 4 sides + 4 corners
Run it. A square! …ish. Hold your corner questions — first, a code smell. 👃

Let them build it the obvious way: select the four motor lines, copy, paste ×3. It runs, and something square-ish appears — do NOT investigate the corners yet; that’s the next act. This slide is about the program: ~16 near-identical lines. Watch for squads that miss a paste or drop a minus sign — those bugs are teaching gold for the quiz that follows.

title: Drive a Square
text:
Quick quiz 🚨

Your square = ~16 copy-pasted lines. Now the judge wants an octagon. Then a triangle. How many lines each time?
Would you bet a competition run on 32 pasted lines?



32 pasted lines — one flipped minus sign from a broken corner. Repeating yourself breeds bugs. Repeats are the computer’s job.

title: Drive a Square
text:
The rescue: repeat with for
for side in range(4):            # do the indented lines 4 times
    print("side number", side)

Run it: prints 0, 1, 2, 3 — computers start counting at 0.
Everything indented under the for is inside the loop.



range(4) = “4 times.” Write it once, repeat it. A square = one corner, four times.

title: Drive a Square
text:
🐛 Bug hunt: the missing colon

This for loop won’t even start — Pybricks flags the first line in red. What’s missing?

for side in range(4)
    left.run_angle(SPEED, SIDE_ANGLE, wait=False)
    right.run_angle(SPEED, SIDE_ANGLE)


SyntaxError — no colon. Every repeat header ends in : — it says “block starts here.” Fix: for side in range(4):



The colon is invisible until it’s missing — run it live so the red SyntaxError lands on the for line. Make the habit loud: say “colon” out loud every time you type a for or while header this week. This is the #1 typo of the day; naming it now saves ten rescues later.

title: Drive a Square
text:
🐛 Bug hunt: the runaway line

The colon’s there now… and it still won’t run. Read the error — it points at line 2.

for side in range(4):
left.run_angle(SPEED, SIDE_ANGLE, wait=False)
right.run_angle(SPEED, SIDE_ANGLE)


IndentationError — lines inside a loop must be pushed in (one Tab). No indent, no “inside.” The spaces tell Python what repeats.



Indentation-as-meaning is the single biggest beginner hurdle — spend a beat. Show both side by side: flush-left (crashes) vs. indented (runs). Then the sneaky version if time allows: indent only line 2 — no crash, but the right wheel fires once, outside the loop. “It ran but it’s wrong” is scarier than a crash, and that’s exactly the lesson.

title: Drive a Square
section: The square — one corner, four times
text:
The square — one corner, four times
SIDE_ANGLE = 391    # same constants as before…
TURN_ANGLE = 148
SPEED = 400

for side in range(4):
    left.run_angle(SPEED, SIDE_ANGLE, wait=False)    # counted → sides are solid
    right.run_angle(SPEED, SIDE_ANGLE)
    left.run_angle(SPEED, TURN_ANGLE, wait=False)    # wheels roll opposite ways…
    right.run_angle(SPEED, -TURN_ANGLE)              # …so the robot spins in place
~16 lines → 8. Octagon? range(8) + one new corner number — two edits.
Now mark your start with tape. Run it three times. Where does it finish?

Close the octagon promise fast: range(8) plus a 45° corner — 45 × 14.5 ÷ 8.8 ≈ 74° — two edits, and a fast squad can try it. Sides use run_angle — yesterday’s tool — so the four sides come out even and repeatable. That’s deliberate: it isolates the failure to the turns. And the 148 is their honest math from this morning, so nobody can blame the number — tires scrub and slip in a pivot, so 148 wheel-degrees ≠ 90 robot-degrees, and it varies run to run. Let them run 3× and watch the last corner miss the tape by a different amount each time.

title: Drive a Square
text:
The square that never closes



The wheels really did roll 148° — your math was right.
But a spinning tire slips → a different angle every run. Counting fixes distance, never heading.
Your robot needs a sense of direction.


⏱️ Time: 25 minutes

title: Drive a Square
text:
You already own this sensor 🧍






The game


Stand up, arms out, eyes closed. Your partner slowly turns you a random amount — maybe a quarter turn, maybe more. Eyes still closed: point at the door.




Most of you will point close. How?! You saw nothing. No wheels counted anything.



Your inner ear feels turning — pure rotation. Robots have it too: the gyro, awake inside the hub this whole time.


⏱️ Time: 5 minutes

title: Drive a Square
text:
One number for “which way”: heading

  
    
    
    
    
    
    0°
    90°
    180°
    -90°
    
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
    
  
  
    
      hub.imu.heading()→ 0°
    
    
      ⟲ Twist left 45°
      ⟳ Twist right 45°
      reset_heading(0)
    
  




heading = degrees turned from where you started. Right = up, left = down. Keep spinning → 360, 450, 720…



Drive the demo from their answers: “if I twist it right a quarter turn, what will the number say?” Click, confirm. “Left half a turn from there?” Click. Then the trap: twist right 8× — the number passes 360 and keeps going. Heading is total turning, not a clock face. reset_heading(0) is the “THIS way is zero” button — they’ll use it in every program today.

title: Drive a Square
text:
What the robot sees


Bridge from the toy demo to the real thing: this readout is real — hub.imu.heading() is one line of code, and 0° is simply wherever the robot pointed when you zeroed it. The next slide puts this exact printout on their own hubs.

title: Drive a Square
text:
Lab: feel the gyro 🖐️
hub.imu.reset_heading(0)        # "THIS way is zero"

for _ in range(100):            # ~10 seconds of just… feeling
    print(hub.imu.heading())
    wait(100)

Pick the robot up. Twist it left, right, all the way around — the numbers follow your hands. No motor moved.
Spin one slow full circle → 360. Keep going → 720!

⏱️ Time: 10 minutes

title: Drive a Square
text:
Quick quiz 🚨

Twist the robot right 90°… then left 90°. What does heading() read now?
90? 180? 0? Something else? Vote first, then try it.



0 — heading is net turning: +90 − 90 = 0. A compass needle, not a step counter.

title: Drive a Square
text:
Plan it in words first

You can left.run(200), left.stop(), and read hub.imu.heading() live. In plain words — how do you turn exactly 90°?
No code. Say the plan out loud.



“Spin. Keep watching the heading. The instant it says 90 — stop.” A turn that watches itself. New trick: repeating while something is true.

title: Drive a Square
text:
New trick: while
while hub.imu.heading() < 90:   # as long as this check is TRUE…
    wait(5)                     # …wait 5 ms, then check again

for repeats a known count. while repeats while a check is true.
This loop does nothing but watch — hundreds of gyro checks per second.



The program parks on the while until the check turns false. “Stare until it’s time” is every robot’s heartbeat.

title: Drive a Square
section: The watched turn — your first gyro move
text:
The watched turn — your first gyro move
hub.imu.reset_heading(0)

left.run(200)                   # left wheel forward…
right.run(-200)                 # …right wheel backward = spin in place
while hub.imu.heading() < 90:   # watch the gyro…
    wait(5)
left.stop()                     # …and stop AT 90. Not "about" 90.
right.stop()
wait(1000)                      # let the coast settle — read too soon and you'll catch it mid-motion
print(hub.imu.heading())        # moment of truth 👀
Run it. What did it print?

Everyone runs it and reports their printed number. Nobody gets exactly 90 — expect 92–96 depending on speed and battery. Don’t explain yet; collect the numbers on the board and let the next slide interrogate them. The wait(1000) before the print is load-bearing, not filler: the robot keeps coasting for a beat after stop(), and reading heading() the instant you call it can catch that motion mid-flight and under-report the overshoot — the settle-wait is what makes the printed number (and the “it printed 94” quiz on the next slide) trustworthy.

title: Drive a Square
text:
Quiz: it printed 94. Who’s guilty? 🚨

The gyro lying? Python too slow? The robot broken?
Hint: try to freeze yourself mid-spin.



Momentum. stop() is instant — a spinning robot isn’t. It coasts past the line, like a car braking. The gyro told the truth: you did end at 94.



Deliberate cliffhanger: the squad leaves with the thief named (momentum) but not yet arrested — the diagnosis is today’s property, the cure is D4’s opening act. If a squad proposes “just ask for 86”, park it with “would 86 survive a battery swap?” and move on; fudge factors are exactly what tomorrow’s fix makes unnecessary. If someone asks why this turn feels jerkier than yesterday’s run_angle glide, it’s the same culprit: no easing off + coast-on-stop() produces both the overshoot number and the lurch — run_angle decelerated into its target, which is why it felt smoother (and still drifted). Fuller version lands on the watched-square slide.

⏱️ Time: 20 minutes

title: Drive a Square
section: The watched square — a loop in a loop
text:
The watched square — a loop in a loop
SIDE_ANGLE = 391    # 30 cm — yesterday's translator
SPEED = 400

for side in range(4):
    left.run_angle(SPEED, SIDE_ANGLE, wait=False)   # side: counted degrees
    right.run_angle(SPEED, SIDE_ANGLE)
    hub.imu.reset_heading(0)                        # corner: watched, not guessed
    left.run(200); right.run(-200)
    while hub.imu.heading() < 90:
        wait(5)
    left.stop(); right.stop()

A while inside a for — count loop draws, watch loop lands each corner.
What’s gone: no 148. The gyro speaks robot-degrees — ask 90, it watches 90.



Tape your start. Run 3× beside the blind square: closer — and off by the same few degrees each time. A bug you can predict is a bug you can fix. 😏




Watch the motion, though: yesterday’s counted square glided — this one lurches to each stop. That’s not the gyro — it’s us flooring it, then cutting power. Smooth ≠ right: the smooth one drifted. Tomorrow — smooth and right. 😏



The day’s two loops, nested — say it out loud: for repeats a known count, while repeats while a check is true, and a real robot program is almost always one inside the other. The comparison is the point: the blind square missed by a different amount each run (scrub and slip), the watched square misses by the same ~4° coast every corner. Consistency is the gyro’s gift — it converts a random bug into a predictable one, which is exactly what D4 exploits.
Field the “but yesterday’s turn was smoother” objection with the purple card, don’t dodge it: run_angle eased off the gas into its target (smooth ride, but slip-blind — it drifted), while this while/stop() turn runs flat-out then coasts (honest heading, rough ride). If a squad says “the calculation looked nicer,” agree — then point at where each square finished: smooth lost on accuracy. The lurch is a driving problem D4 cures (arrive slowly, then a controller), not a gyro problem — do NOT preview the fix, just name that it’s coming.

title: Drive a Square
text:
Pro move: targets that climb 🧗

Each corner resets heading to 0. But heading keeps counting: 360, 450, 720… What if you never reset — aim corner 1 at 90, corner 2 at 180, corner 3 at 270?
Where would the coast go?


hub.imu.reset_heading(0)                    # once, before the loop
for side in range(4):
    left.run_angle(SPEED, SIDE_ANGLE, wait=False)
    right.run_angle(SPEED, SIDE_ANGLE)
    left.run(200); right.run(-200)
    while hub.imu.heading() < 90 * (side + 1):   # 90, 180, 270, 360
        wait(5)
    left.stop(); right.stop()



The target is built from the loop variable — so the coast stops piling up: overshoot to 94, and corner 2 has 4° less to turn. Each mistake pays for the next. Fast squads: race both squares — which closes tighter?



Stretch material — gate nobody on it. It’s the payoff of the earlier “keep spinning → 360, 450, 720” trap AND the first time an expression (90 * (side + 1)) does work inside a loop. The insight — absolute targets absorb per-corner error instead of accumulating it — is a real WRO technique they’ll meet again in mission code. If time is short, demo it coach-side in 2 minutes, then move on — the next slide (Any Shape) is the one that feeds Shape Shifter.

title: Drive a Square
text:
Any shape, two numbers 🔺⬣


  
    
    
    
    
      
      
    
  
  
    
      
      
      0°
      of turning
    
    
      sides = –
    
    
    
      Triangle
      Square
      Pentagon
      Hexagon
      Octagon
    
  





Every corner adds up to one full lap is 360°, so corner = 360 ÷ sides.

A new shape in the code is just two edits: the range(...) count and that corner angle.


Elicit the 360 with bodies first, not algebra: one student walks a triangle on the mat and ends facing the way they started — however many corners, the turning always totals one full spin. Then drive the demo from their guesses: pick Triangle, each corner flashes its 120° turn right on the shape while the dial climbs 120 → 240 → 360 and closes; pick Octagon, eight skinny 45s stack to the same 360. That “always lands on 360” is the whole proof — corner = 360 ÷ n falls out of it. The contrast with the morning is worth a beat: same octagon, but the gyro eats robot-degrees directly, so the gap ÷ wheel translation retires (it returns at the D10 reveal inside DriveBase). Every squad edits their own file to a triangle and runs it before Shape Shifter.

⏱️ Time: 20 minutes

title: Drive a Square
text:
Shape Shifter 🔺⬛⬣






The challenge


Judge calls a shape — triangle up to octagon. 60 seconds to edit and run: range(?) + corner = 360 ÷ sides. Score = gap from tape to finish; tiebreak = fewest lines touched. 3 calls, best two count.




Two edits per shape — that’s the whole race. Copy-pasters meet their doom at the octagon. 😈
Pro question: which drifts more — 3 fat corners or 8 skinny ones? Watch your traces; keep the answer for tomorrow.


Uses exactly today’s material: for + range, the watched-turn while, 360 ÷ n, and heading — no def; that arrives tomorrow. The 60-second edit window is the point: a loop makes a new shape a two-number change, so the octagon call separates loopers from pasters on the clock. The closure gap keeps the coast visible — 8 corners × ~4° beats 3 × ~4°, so the octagon drifts more; squads that see that are already leaning into tomorrow’s cure. Coach scorecard: edit discipline (two calm edits vs. a rewrite), 360 ÷ n reasoning without a calculator, and who re-tapes and re-tests between calls.

title: Drive a Square
text:
Today’s gate






You’re done today when…


The judge calls any shape → your loop draws it with watched corners (two edits, no copy-paste). You can explain heading, walk your watched turn line by line, and name who’s guilty for the 94.




Tomorrow: the coast meets its cure — arrive slowly. Then the cure gets a brain: speed that shrinks with the error. One formula… and it has a name every roboticist on Earth knows.

title: Make It Move
text:
Before we code

🏗️ Build catch-up — didn’t finish yesterday? Finish now, no penalty.
✅ Re-run the four checks: click · twin · shake · roll.
🔋 Battery light green-ish? You’ll see why that matters today.

⏱️ Time: 20 minutes

title: Make It Move
text:
Unplugged: drive a blindfolded partner






The game


One of you is the programmer; your blindfolded partner is the robot. Only discrete commands — “forward 30 cm,” “turn 90° right.” No steering, no touching. Get your robot across the mat to the target.




Give the commands. Watch where your “robot” actually ends up.
Swap roles and run it again.

⏱️ Time: 15 minutes

title: Make It Move
text:
What just went wrong?


You commanded

forward 30 cm
turn 90°
forward 30 cm


The robot did

forward ~34 cm
turn ~80°
forward 30 cm… into the wrong spot




That gap is open-loop drift — moving without checking the result. Hold that thought: your LEGO robot is about to do exactly the same thing.

title: Make It Move
text:
🏁 The race

  
    🏁 Race!
    ⏱ 0.0 s
  
  
    🚗
    6 m in 3 s
  
  
    🚙
    3 m in 3 s
  
  




Same start. Same 3 seconds. What did you notice?




…and how much faster is 🚗?




“A lot” is not a measurement.



Run the race two or three times. Elicit, don’t tell: “What did you notice?” (Car A got farther / arrived first.) “Yes — what do we call that?” SPEED. “You’re scientists now — and scientists need an accurate way to describe speed. Suggestions?” Guide toward: how far it went… and how long it took. The moment someone says “distance and time,” celebrate it — the next slide is their idea, formalized.

title: Make It Move
text:
Say it with numbers


🚗 Car A: 6 m in 3 s
→ 6 ÷ 3 = 2 m every second

🚙 Car B: 3 m in 3 s
→ 3 ÷ 3 = 1 m every second



speed = distance ÷ time — how far you get, per second. That’s the whole formula. You invented it.



Anchor with everyday speeds: a car does 100 km/h — 100 km of distance for every hour. Ask: what’s YOUR walking speed? (~5 km/h ≈ 1.4 m/s.) Then shrink to robot scale: centimeters per second.

title: Make It Move
text:
Make it move LAB
from pybricks.hubs import PrimeHub
from pybricks.pupdevices import Motor
from pybricks.parameters import Port, Direction
from pybricks.tools import wait

hub = PrimeHub()
left = Motor(Port.A, Direction.COUNTERCLOCKWISE)
right = Motor(Port.E)

left.run_time(500, 2000, wait=False)
right.run_time(500, 2000)

Walk the highlight steps live: (1) the imports pull in the hub/motor/port tools; (2) we name each motor by the port it’s plugged into — the left wheel spins backward, so left gets COUNTERCLOCKWISE; (3) run_time(500, 2000) runs the motor for 2000 ms, and wait=False lets the left motor start while the right one is told to go, so both move together. Don’t explain the 500 yet — they just defined speed, and the next slide interrogates it.

title: Make It Move
text:
🐛 Bug hunt

This program crashes before the robot twitches. Find the crime.

from pybricks.parameters import Port, Direction

left = Motor(Port.A, Direction.COUNTERCLOCKWISE)
right = Motor(Port.E)

left.run_time(500, 2000, wait=False)
right.run_time(500, 2000)


NameError: name 'Motor' is not defined — we never imported Motor. Python only knows the tools you pull in: from pybricks.pupdevices import Motor.



Run each buggy program live on one robot if possible — the error message on screen is the payoff. Teams shout the line number, not the fix, first. Reading broken code is far less scary than writing fresh code; that’s the whole point of this warm-up.

title: Make It Move
text:
🐛 Bug hunt

The imports are perfect… and it still crashes. Why?

from pybricks.pupdevices import Motor
from pybricks.parameters import Port, Direction

left = Motor(Port.A, Direction.COUNTERCLOCKWISE)
right = Motor(Port.B)

left.run_time(500, 2000, wait=False)
right.run_time(500, 2000)


OSError — the code says Port B, but the right motor is plugged into Port E. The program must match the physical robot. Check the cable, then check the letter.

title: Make It Move
text:
🥌 Game 1: Robot Curling






The rules


Tape three zones on the floor: 10 pts · 25 pts · 50 pts bullseye. Start behind the line, run your program, hands off. Robot must stop fully inside a zone — slide past the last zone and it’s 0. You may change only one number: the 2000. 3 throws, best one counts.



left.run_time(500, 2000, wait=False)   # ← tune ONLY the time
right.run_time(500, 2000)


Throw 1 lands short, throw 2 with the same number lands somewhere else?! Keep your score sheet — that wobble is evidence, and we’ll need it later today. 🕵️


⏱️ Time: 15 minutes

Zones at roughly 80 / 110 / 140 cm from the start line — close enough that ~2–3 s runs land in range, far enough that guessing badly is visible. Hand out the score sheet; make them write every DRIVE_TIME they try and where it stopped. That log is the raw material for the speed lab later — don’t tell them yet.

title: Make It Move
section: 🅿️ Bonus round: The Price Is Right
text:
🅿️ Bonus round: The Price Is Right






The rules


Park your robot’s nose as close to the wall as you can without touching it. Touch = 0. Judge measures the gap with a ruler — smallest gap wins.




Same law as curling: tune the time, nothing else.
Pro tip: sneak up in two runs? Not allowed — one program, one shot. 😏

⏱️ Time: 10 minutes — if the curling podium is settled

title: Make It Move
text:
Quick quiz 🚨

run_time(500, 2000) — you just defined speed… so 500 is a speed of what? And the 2000?
Centimeters per second? Meters? Robot points? Guess!



2000 = milliseconds — how long it runs. 500 = degrees per second. It is a speed — something ÷ time — but the something isn’t distance… 🤔



Ask “what speed did you just use?” — expect silence; that’s the plan. Reveal: it’s a different kind of speed. Pybricks motor commands really do take rotational (angular) speed in deg/s — that’s the motor’s own language. The next slide makes them derive it.

title: Make It Move
text:
⚙️ Motors have a speed too

  
    Motor A
    
      
    
    0.0 rotations
  
  
    ▶ Spin for 3 s
  
  
    Motor B
    
      
    
    0.0 rotations
  




No road, no distance — so count what a motor does: turns. Spin speed = rotations ÷ time. Same recipe, new ingredient.



Elicit: “This is a motor — how can we measure how fast it is?” Expect “rotations per second” — agree enthusiastically. Don’t reveal degrees yet! Ask: “But what if I want to spin only half a turn? Or a quarter?” — let them sit with that before the next slide.

title: Make It Move
section: 🧭 What about half a rotation? A quarter?
text:
🧭 What about half a rotation? A quarter?


  
  
    
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
    
    
      0°
    
    
      = 0 rotation
    
  

  
  
    0°
    90° — ¼ turn
    180° — ½ turn
    270° — ¾ turn
    360° — full turn
  





0, 30, 60, 90 … 360 — ancient sky-watchers sliced a full circle into 360 equal steps called degrees. The idea comes from the Babylonians (~2000 BCE), who loved multiples of 60. The Greeks inherited and spread it, and we are still using it today! 🏛️



Ask: “If I want exactly half a turn, how do I write that as a number of full rotations?” (0.5 — messy fractions ahead!) Reveal: that’s why we use degrees. Walk through the dial: 90° = quarter, 180° = half, 360° = full. Then fun history: Babylonians, base-60, ~360 days in a year — they made 360 the magic number. Greeks (Hipparchus, Ptolemy) adopted and spread it. LEGO engineers still follow that 4000-year-old choice.

title: Make It Move
text:
Name your numbers — variables
DRIVE_SPEED = 500   # deg/s
DRIVE_TIME = 2000   # ms

left.run_time(DRIVE_SPEED, DRIVE_TIME, wait=False)
right.run_time(DRIVE_SPEED, DRIVE_TIME)

Name a number once → change it in one place → every move retunes. First habit of clean robot code.

title: Make It Move
text:
🎯 Mission: drive exactly 100 cm

Your robot only listens to deg/s and ms. How do you order it to travel 100 cm?
Think: what’s the only part of the robot that actually touches the mat?



The wheel 🛞 — every degree the motor turns becomes distance on the ground. Let’s see exactly how much.

title: Make It Move
text:
🛞 Unroll the wheel


  Tiny wheel — rope around the rim = 8 cm
  
  
  
    
      
      
      
    
  
  
  
  1 rotation = the whole rope = 8 cm

  YOUR wheel — rope around the rim ≈ 27.6 cm
  
  
  
    
      
      
      
    
  
  
  
  1 rotation = the whole rope ≈ 27.6 cm

🛞 Roll one full turn



The rim is a rope wrapped in a circle — rolling one turn unfolds the rope flat on the ground. A wheel whose rope is 8 cm travels exactly 8 cm per rotation; your big wheel’s rope is ≈ 27.6 cm, so one rotation = 27.6 cm. Ask before rolling: “if the rope is 8 cm, how far does one rotation take you?” (Note: the two scenes use different zoom levels — each ruler is in its own wheel’s units.)

title: Make It Move
text:
From deg/s to cm/s



The Translation
Since 1 full rotation is 360° = 27.6 cm: \[360\text{ deg/s} = 27.6\text{ cm/s}\]

To convert any speed, we scale it: \[\text{Speed (cm/s)} = \text{Speed (deg/s)} \times \frac{27.6}{360}\]



Practical Example 🧮
For DRIVE_SPEED = 500:

\(500 \times \frac{27.6}{360} \approx 38.3\text{ cm/s}\)

Let’s check that claim!

title: Make It Move
text:
🔬 Speed lab — measure YOUR robot

Put tape at the front wheels. Run the program: DRIVE_SPEED = 500, DRIVE_TIME = 2000.
Measure the distance with a ruler. Write it down.
Compute: speed = distance ÷ 2 seconds. Math predicted ~38 cm/s → ~77 cm.



Got less than 77? And your neighbor got a different number — same code?! (Hint: check both battery lights. 🔋) Commanding is not the same as getting.


⏱️ Time: 15 minutes

title: Make It Move
text:
So… exactly 100 cm?

At ~38 cm/s: time = 100 ÷ 38 ≈ 2.6 s. Set DRIVE_TIME = 2600. Run it. Measure it.
Did anyone land on 100 cm?



Short again — and different every run. Timed driving trusts the battery to keep its promise. There has to be a better way…


⏱️ Time: 10 minutes

title: Make It Move
text:
run_time vs run_angle


Live demo if time allows: run the same run_time program on a full hub and a nearly-empty hub — different distances. Then run_angle on both — same distance. The encoder inside the motor counts degrees; time doesn’t lie the same way twice.

title: Make It Move
text:
🎯 Predict-a-distance

You want exactly 100 cm. One wheel turn (360°) ≈ 27.6 cm. How many degrees?


🧮 Direct Conversion

Degrees per 1 cm:   \(1\text{ cm} = \frac{360^\circ}{27.6\text{ cm}} \approx 13.04^\circ/\text{cm}\)
For 100 cm:   \(\text{degrees} = 100\text{ cm} \times 13.04^\circ/\text{cm} \approx \mathbf{1304^\circ}\)



🤖 Try It Out
# Spin left and right motors together to go 100 cm
left.run_angle(DRIVE_SPEED, 1304, wait=False)
right.run_angle(DRIVE_SPEED, 1304)

⏱️ Time: 10 minutes

title: Make It Move
text:
Diameter → circumference



circumference = π × diameter. Your wheel: 3.14 × 8.8 cm ≈ 27.6 cm. Any wheel on Earth: read the diameter off the tire, multiply by π, and you speak its language.

title: Make It Move
text:
🧪 Bonus: discover π yourself

Wrap a string around anything round — cup, tape roll, bottle, your wheel. That’s C.
Measure straight across the middle. That’s d.
Divide: C ÷ d = ?



≈ 3.14. Every. Single. Time. That number is π — the circle’s built-in constant. You didn’t memorize it; you discovered it.

title: Make It Move
section: 🧪 Bonus: another way to measure a circle
text:
🧪 Bonus: another way to measure a circle
No string? Catch the circle with straight lines instead.

  
    
      
    
    
    
    d = 1
    
      
    
  
  
    A square's rope needs no π:perimeter = 4 × side
    
      Outside square: 4 × 1 = 4
      → circle's rope is less than 4
    
    
      Inside square: rope ≈ 2.83
      → circle's rope is more than 2.83
    
  



2.83 < π < 4 — trapped, no string needed! A rough trap, though… how do we squeeze tighter? More sides →



Optional enrichment — no gate depends on it; skip live if time is tight and point kids to it for home. Build: squares are the shapes we can measure (4 × side, no π). Click 1: outside square (side = d = 1) → its rope is 4, and the circle’s rope is clearly shorter. Click 2: inside square → rope ≈ 2.83, circle clearly longer. Don’t derive the 2.83 (side = d/√2) — the picture is the proof. Since d = 1, rope ÷ d IS π, so the trap holds π itself. The “more sides” question is the cliffhanger into the demo.

title: Make It Move
text:
🧪 Bonus: Archimedes’ squeeze

  
    
    
    
    
    d = 1
  
  
    
      4
      6
      12
      24
      96
    
    4 sides
    
      2.8284
       < π < 
      4.0000
    
    real π = 3.14159…
  




Archimedes (Syracuse, ~250 BCE) did exactly this by hand — doubling up to 96 sides — and proved 3 10/71 < π < 3 1/7. He squeezed π out of a circle 2,200 years before calculators. 🏛️



Optional enrichment, self-guided at home. The inner rope (teal) is always too short, the outer (coral) always too long — the circle’s rope is trapped between them, and since d = 1 the trap holds π itself. Click 4 → 96 and watch both numbers march toward 3.14159. At 96 sides the trap is 3.1410–3.1427 — Archimedes’ actual stopping point. Connect back: the Babylonians gave us degrees, Archimedes gave us π — both live inside every wheel calculation the robot does.

title: Make It Move
text:
Radar Gun 📡






The challenge


Predict, then prove. Measure your robot’s speed, then tell the judge exactly how many seconds it needs to cross the 1.5 m finish line. Judge starts the stopwatch — closest prediction wins. 3 attempts, best counts.




Pure science: measure → calculate → predict → test.
Pro move: does your prediction survive a battery swap? 😏

title: Make It Move
text:
Today’s gate






You’re done today when…


Your robot moves with named variables, you can convert deg/s → cm/s for your wheel, and you can explain why counted degrees beat timed seconds.




Next: Drive a Square — meet the gyro, a hidden sense you’ll feel with your own hands, and watch it snap tomorrow’s open-loop square shut.

title: Make It Move
text:
Tonight’s mission 🎬

You made it move. You measured it like a scientist.

Optional at home: WRO-Learn — Fundamentals of Mechanics (short videos — pure enrichment; everything you need is delivered in class).

title: Name Your Moves
text:
Where we left off







Yesterday
The trick




📈 P, named
speed = error × gain — far → fast, close → gentle, there → zero


🔁 one formula, two jobs
the watched turn and the self-healing straight — same line, two uses


🔥 crack #1
gain = 20 flew past the target and zig-zagged back


😴 crack #2
gain = 1 parked a hair short — a leftover P never quite killed





Both cracks survived yesterday’s gain quiz — each one is a letter we collect this morning
Then every move you own gets a name in your own library


⏱️ Time: 10 minutes

Recap by asking, not telling: “walk me through gyro_turn_p — what does gain do at a big error vs a tiny one?” Then surface the two cracks they already saw on the D4 quiz: the hot gain overshot and rang; the lazy gain parked short. Don’t fix anything yet — name that the morning’s job is those two leftovers, which turn out to be the last two letters of PID, and the afternoon’s job is turning everything they’ve hand-built into named library functions. Keep P as the hero they built; D and I are its finishing touches, not a new subject.

title: Name Your Moves
section: Break it: P in a hurry 🔥
text:
Break it: P in a hurry 🔥

  
    ▶ gyro_turn_p(90) — gain cranked 🔥
  
  
    
      
      
      
      
    
    
      heading = 0°  target 90°
      
        
        
      
      P sprints in… and slams the door. 🚪
    
  




The push only knows how far is left — not how fast it’s closing
It arrives at 90 flat-out, blows past, over-corrects, and rings before it settles
A bigger gain rings harder



Run it before the fragment. The red arc painted past the tick is the overshoot made physical — point at it. Elicit the diagnosis in their words: “the number hit 90 — why didn’t it stop?” Because the wheels were still spinning fast; P read error 0 and cut power, but momentum carried it past, then P yanked it back, and round it goes. This is exactly the D3 coast — but now P can’t two-speed its way out, because a hot gain is what won the race yesterday. Set up the need: we want something that notices the error is dropping fast and eases off early. Don’t name D yet.

title: Name Your Moves
text:
Name it: D damps 🛑
def gyro_turn_pd(target, gain=4, kd=0.5):
    hub.imu.reset_heading(0)
    last_error = target
    while abs(target - hub.imu.heading()) > 1:
        error = target - hub.imu.heading()   # how FAR → P
        change = (error - last_error) / 0.005 # how FAST → D (°/s)
        last_error = error
        left.run(gain * error + kd * change) # ease off early
        right.run(-(gain * error + kd * change))
        wait(5)
    left.stop(); right.stop()


P reads where you ARE. D reads how fast you’re closing
Watch error fall 90 → 55 → 30: each tick change is negative (−35, then −25) — the gap is closing fast
D catches that and eases off early — it taps the brake before you arrive, so the turn glides in instead of ringing. That’s D — derivative, the second letter of PID



Anticipation is the whole idea — say it that way: P only knows the gap’s size right now; D knows the gap is shrinking and lifts off early, like braking as you near a parking spot instead of at it. Three new lines: remember last tick’s error, subtract to get the change (a speed, °/s), feed it in. Sign check out loud on the card’s numbers — as the robot closes, error shrinks each tick, so change is negative, so it trims the command down → gentle arrival. kd is a new knob, same as gain was — a strength dial for the braking. Too little kd = still rings (P alone); too much = it brakes so early it crawls in sluggishly. They’ll feel both on the next slide. gyro_turn_pd stays on run() (see the D4 proportional-turn note on run() vs motor.dc()); worth knowing for coaches — and it pays off again at the D10 reveal, where the pro controller actuates one layer deeper, by torque/effort.

title: Name Your Moves
text:
Feel it: same turn, calmed 🎢

  
    ▶ race P vs PD to 90°
    kd: 0.5
    
  
  
    
      
        
        
        
        
        
      
      🔴 P — big car · 🟢 PD — small car
    
    
      
      
      heading
      time →
      
      target 90°
      
      
      🔴 P alone — rings
      🟢 PD — glides in
      
      
      you are HERE
    
  
  
    P landed –° · PD landed –°
  




Same hot gain, same target. P reads only the error’s size and rings — watch the red car paint its wobble past the tick
PD also reads its speed and eases off early — one clean glide (the graph is the cars’ diary; the yellow dot is now)
Slide kd to 0 and the green curve rings too; too high and it crawls in. Fast when far, gentle as it arrives.



Reading ritual first, same as yesterday’s dot-and-car slides: the two cars on the dial ARE the robots (big red = P, small green = PD); the graph is their diary, written left to right while they turn; the yellow dot at each trace’s tip is now — car and dot move together, same robot, two views. Run at kd=0.5 — narrate the dial (“red blows past the tick, paints the arc, swings back; green glides on”) then point at the diary telling the identical story. Drag kd to 0 live: the green curve collapses onto the red one — proof that D is the only difference. Nudge kd up to ~1.5: green now approaches sluggishly and arrives late. The sweet spot is theirs to feel, exactly like the gain knob yesterday. The sim is illustrative, not gospel — on the real robot they’ll tune kd by eye, same habit.

title: Name Your Moves
text:
The last letter: I — (coach demo) 🧲

Rest a thumb of weight on one side. Now the P-turn stops a hair short — 88°, not 90 — and stays there, forever. Why can’t P finish the job?


  
    ▶ drive into the lean
    I-term: OFF
  
  
    
      
        
        
        
        🧲
      
      🧲 = the lean, riding the car
    
    
      
      
      heading
      time →
      
      target 90°
      
      stuck 5° short 😤
      
      you are HERE
    
  
  
    the leftover P can't push out, piling up in I's memory:
    
  




P’s push (gain × error) can’t beat the lean, so it parks a hair short forever — that’s yesterday’s gain = 1, now explained
Turn I ON: each tick piles the leftover onto a running total (the purple bar) until the sum beats the lean. I — integral



This is a coach demo — never gated. Show it, don’t assign it. Point at the dial first: the car (🧲 riding its side — that’s the thumb of weight) climbs and sticks short of the tick, and the red sliver between nose and tick IS the flatline gap on the diary next to it — same robot, two views, yellow dot = now. Toggle I OFF first: the purple curve rises then flatlines short, the “stuck 5° short” tag appears — that’s yesterday’s gain=1 parks-short, now explained. Toggle I ON and rerun: the accumulator bar fills, the car creeps the last sliver onto the tick, and the curve creeps up to 90. Say plainly why it barely matters for us: our mission moves are short — a 30 cm straight or a 90° turn ends before the accumulator fills — so I earns its seat on long steady offsets, not here. They now own the whole acronym; that’s the win, not a tuned I.

title: Name Your Moves
text:
You own all three letters 🏆

  
    P · I · D
  


  
    P
    the error's sizefar → hard push
  
  
    I
    the error's historykills a steady lean
  
  
    D
    the error's speedbrakes before arrival
  



Size, history, speed — three readings of one number, the error
This exact trio runs inside every drone, thermostat, and Mars rover
You didn’t learn a LEGO trick; you finished the most-used formula in robotics. Now give your moves names. 📦



Let the acronym land with a beat of silence — this is the payoff of two days. Say it in one breath: P built yesterday, D and I today, all three read the same error different ways. Then pivot: every one of those moves still lives as a loose pasted loop in one file. The afternoon turns them into a library — named functions they’ll carry all the way to the competition table.

title: Name Your Moves
text:
Break it: the copy-paste swamp 🔥

The judge calls: turn to 90 · drive 40 cm · turn to 45 · drive 30 cm. With yesterday’s file, how many lines do you paste — and when a bug turns up in the turn loop, in how many places do you fix it?


  
    turn to 90
    while abs(90 - …) > 1:
      error = 90 - …
      left.run(4*error + …)
      … 5 more lines …
    🐛 fix here…
  
  
    drive 40 cm
    while left.angle() < …:
      error = heading()
      left.run(500 - 4*error)
      … 5 more lines …
    …and here…
  
  
    turn to 45
    while abs(45 - …) > 1:
      error = 45 - …
      left.run(4*error + …)
      … 5 more lines …
    …and here…
  
  
    drive 30 cm
    while left.angle() < …:
      error = heading()
      left.run(500 - 4*error)
      … 5 more lines …
    …and here. 😵
  



Four moves ≈ 30 pasted lines, and one bug means four fixes — miss one and the robot lies to you
A real mission has a dozen moves. The cure isn’t more pasting — it’s naming: write each move once, call it forever



Let them actually start pasting for the judge’s call — a minute of real pasting sells the swamp better than any slide. Then freeze: “you already know the cure — you used it yesterday.” def gyro_turn was the first named move; today every move gets a name, in a file of its own. Vocabulary to land: a library = a file of named moves; your mission file imports the verbs. This is the same trick run_angle plays — someone at LEGO named a move so you wouldn’t have to paste motor physics.

title: Name Your Moves
section: Fix it: a file of your own — my_lib.py 📦
text:
Fix it: a file of your own — my_lib.py 📦
# my_lib.py — YOUR moves: written once, named forever
from pybricks.hubs import PrimeHub
from pybricks.pupdevices import Motor
from pybricks.parameters import Port, Direction
from pybricks.tools import wait

hub = PrimeHub()
left = Motor(Port.A, Direction.COUNTERCLOCKWISE)
right = Motor(Port.E)

KP = 4                                # named constants — tune HERE,
WHEEL_MM = 88                         # nowhere else
DEG_PER_MM = 360 / (3.14 * WHEEL_MM)  # Day 2's wheel math, bottled


One new file. The top holds the robot’s facts (ports, gains, wheel math); below it live the verbs
Your mission file just says from my_lib import drive_straight, turn_to — and reads like a story instead of a swamp



Make the file for real, together — everyone creates my_lib.py next to their mission file. The constants block is the same discipline from the square day, now promoted to the library’s front door: facts at the top, verbs below, missions elsewhere. DEG_PER_MM bottles the D2 rope-unroll math (360° per wheel-turn ÷ circumference) so distance-in-mm is a multiply away. Two verbs move in today; the library keeps growing all week — wall verbs next session, line verbs after — same file, more names.

title: Name Your Moves
section: Verb #1: turn_to — the PD turn, named
text:
Verb #1: turn_to — the PD turn, named
def turn_to(target, gain=4, kd=0.5):
    """PD gyro turn to a heading ON THE MAT — no reset."""
    # hub.imu.reset_heading(0)   ← DELETED. That's the whole diff.
    last_error = target - hub.imu.heading()
    ...   # the rest IS gyro_turn_pd, line for line


Copy gyro_turn_pd into my_lib.py, rename it, delete the reset — that’s the whole edit
turn_to(90) now means face 90 on the mat — not “turn 90 more”
Arrive at a corner 1° hot? The next turn_to absorbs it. Errors stop stacking.



The dropped reset is the day’s sneakiest upgrade — walk it slowly. With reset, every turn measures from wherever the robot happens to point, so corner errors add up (the D3 square never closed for exactly this reason). Without reset, the gyro keeps one map-wide heading and every turn_to is an absolute appointment: 90 is 90, whatever mess came before. Quiz it: “you’re at heading 92 after a bumped corner — what does turn_to(180) do?” (turns 88, not 90 — it fixes the bump for free). The gyro heading keeps counting past 360, which the square slide uses. Defaults (gain=4, kd=0.5) are their tuned D4/D5 values — theirs may differ; that’s the point of owning the knobs. Don’t unpack the = in the def line yet — default gets named and drilled two slides on.

title: Name Your Moves
section: Verb #2: drive_straight — a straight with a finish line
text:
Verb #2: drive_straight — a straight with a finish line
def drive_straight(distance_mm, speed=400):
    """Drive distance_mm, healing the heading the whole way."""
    heading = hub.imu.heading()                    # lock THIS heading
    finish = left.angle() + distance_mm * DEG_PER_MM
    while left.angle() < finish:                   # encoder finish line
        error = hub.imu.heading() - heading        # one heartbeat
        left.run(speed - KP * error)
        right.run(speed + KP * error)
        wait(5)
    left.stop(); right.stop()


Day 2’s wheel math sets the finish line in degrees; Day 4’s healing loop steers every heartbeat on the way there
A verb calling a verb would stack — here it’s inlined for simplicity, same idea: libraries grow by stacking, not pasting



Single function now: drive_straight handles both the finish line (encoder degrees) and the healing loop (P steering each tick). finish is measured from wherever the wheel already is (no encoder reset), same absolute-not-relative discipline as turn_to. Backwards moves (a negative distance_mm) need the comparison flipped — park it as a challenge today; it becomes urgent at the wall session’s measured back-off, and the coach exemplar handles it. Benchmarks come at the gate: 1 m within ±2 cm is very reachable with a measured wheel. The speed=400 in the def line gets its name — default — on the very next slide.

title: Name Your Moves
text:
Knobs with defaults 🎛️
def turn_to(target, gain=4, kd=0.5):         # two knobs, factory-set
def drive_straight(distance_mm, speed=400):  # one knob, factory-set

Every = in a def line is a default — a factory setting. Skip the knob, the factory value runs; name it — a keyword argument like kd=0 — and you turn that one knob, nothing else


Predict — what runs? ① turn_to(90) · ② turn_to(90, kd=0) · ③ drive_straight(300, speed=150) · ④ drive_straight(speed=150)



① all factory settings — your tuned gain=4, kd=0.5 run unasked · ② brake knob to zero — yesterday’s ringing P is back, on demand 🔔




③ one careful straight at 150 — every other call still fast · ④ 💥 TypeError — distance_mm has no default. Some knobs must be turned



Name the vocabulary plainly, once: the names in the parentheses are parameters; a parameter with = carries a default; naming a knob at the call site (kd=0) is a keyword argument. They’ve used all three since gyro_turn_p(target, gain=4) — this slide only hangs names on habits. Quiz cadence: read a call, every squad commits out loud, then reveal the card — ② is the fun one (one keyword resurrects yesterday’s ringing bug, proof the default was doing real work), ④ is the confidence move: run it live, read the TypeError together — the interpreter is telling you which knob you forgot, not scolding you. Forward hook for coaches: at the D10 reveal, RobotConfig gathers all these factory settings into one dataclass — same idea, bigger box.

title: Name Your Moves
text:
Two homes for a name 🗺️
# top of my_lib.py — GLOBALS: born once, every verb reads them
hub = PrimeHub()
KP = 4

def drive_straight(distance_mm, speed=400):
    heading = hub.imu.heading()              # LOCALS: born at the call…
    error = hub.imu.heading() - heading      # …gone the moment it ends

The run just ended — what does print(error) print? And: change KP = 4 → 6 at the top — how many verbs feel it?



print(error) → NameError — error is a local: born at the call, dead at the end. A feature — every verb gets private scratch names that never collide




KP is a global — one edit, every verb feels it. The file’s whole shape: facts up top (global) · verbs below · scratch inside (local)



Two words, two homes — point at the actual file while saying it: a name born at the top of the file is a global (born once, when the file loads; every verb below can read it — hub, left, KP, DEG_PER_MM); a name born inside a def is a local (born fresh at every call, gone at the end — heading, finish, error). Run the print(error) NameError live if a laptop’s handy; kids expect the number to still be there, and the error landing is the lesson. Then sell dying-locals as a feature: turn_to and drive_straight both use error and never trip over each other, and every call starts with a clean slate. The KP question is the payoff of the “tune HERE, nowhere else” comment they already wrote — one global, read by everyone, edited in one place. Don’t mention the global keyword; the next slide handles the one trap without it.

title: Name Your Moves
section: Break it: the tune that didn’t stick 🔥
text:
Break it: the tune that didn’t stick 🔥
def make_it_sharper():
    KP = 12          # crank steering for the whole library… right?

make_it_sharper()
drive_straight(300)  # …still lazy. Nothing changed. 😤


Assigning a name inside a def births a brand-new local — even when a global already owns that name. This KP lived inside make_it_sharper… and died at the end of the call. The top-of-file KP never moved
The fix is zero new code — it’s the rule the file already taught you: read a global from anywhere; change it only at the top. KP = 12 belongs in exactly ONE place — the constants block



Act it out before explaining: type make_it_sharper live, run the straight, watch nothing change — let the 😤 be theirs. Then the one-sentence rule: the moment you assign a name inside a def, Python births a new local, even if a global already wears that name — the global is untouched, and the local evaporates at return. (Inside make_it_sharper the new KP even hides the global — same name, different box.) Do not teach the global keyword as the escape hatch — the library discipline is strictly better and it’s already on the slide: constants change at the top, nowhere else. Coach’s radar for Weeks 2–3: when a squad swears “I tuned it and it didn’t stick,” the first question is “where did you assign it?” — this slide is that bug, met safely in advance.

title: Name Your Moves
text:
The square, in four names
from my_lib import drive_straight, turn_to

def drive_square(side_mm):
    for corner in [90, 180, 270, 360]:
        drive_straight(side_mm)
        turn_to(corner)

drive_square(300)


Run it. The square snaps shut.
Each corner is an appointment on the mat — 90, 180, 270, 360 — so a hot corner can’t poison the next one
Day 3’s drift didn’t get patched; it became impossible. (And notice what the mission just turned into: a list. 😉)



Close the loop hard: put Day 3’s taped drift-square next to this one. Everything converged — for repeats the move, the library names it, PD watches it, and absolute headings stop the corner errors stacking (the heading counts past 360, which is why the last appointment is 360 and not 0). The [90, 180, 270, 360] list is a deliberate wink — the mission-runner session turns whole missions into exactly this shape; don’t teach lists now, just let the shape sit there. Show off with drive_square(200), drive_square(400).

Debuggability is the library’s superpower, and it’s the skill Week 3’s mission work leans on.

text:
Your turn: own the knobs 🏋️







challenge

say which knob you turned — and what came back 🔔

Fast squads: invent wiggle(times=3) — a new verb with a factory setting of its own 🐍

The finger-first rule on 4️⃣ matters: deciding where an edit belongs is the whole global/local/default lesson compressed into one gesture. Fast-squad wiggle(times=3): a for-loop of two quick opposite turn_tos is plenty — the point is authoring a def line with a default of their own.

text:
Bullseye 🎯

Drive forward exactly 1 metre with your own drive_straight and stop as close to the mark as you can. 3 attempts, best counts. (Fast squads: Sniper — 3 mat targets, score = total distance error.)

Pro move: does your run survive a battery swap? A judge’s evil nudge mid-run? (It should — the healing loop is yours. 😏) The red ghost is where a guessed diameter would stop.


The competition proves their code, not a shortcut — that’s the day’s whole point, so say it: the button reads drive_straight, the function they wrote an hour ago. Battery swap and nudge are the two classic fakes that expose open-loop code; their encoder finish line shrugs off the battery (degrees are degrees at any voltage) and their P-loop shrugs off the nudge. title: Name Your Moves
text:
Today’s gate






You’re done today when…


Your own drive_straight(1000) lands 1.0 m within ±2 cm and turn_to lands within ±2°, across 10 trials — on code you wrote line by line — your drive_square() (four library calls) closes the Day-3 square, and you can say what D and I each add to P.




Next: Stop at the Wall — the motor becomes a sensor, and your library grows its first sensing verbs.


The benchmarks are the plan’s Week-1 gate verbatim: drive_straight 1.0 m ±2 cm, turn_to ±2° over 10 trials, square closed, D and I explained in their own words (I is never gated as a build — it was a coach demo). Log each student’s pass in the scorecard; these same benchmarks are re-run at the end-of-week-2 gate, so a shaky pass today is a flag, not a fail. title: The Drive That Heals Itself
text:
Where we left off







Yesterday
The trick




🧭 heading
one number for “which way” — net turning since reset_heading(0)


👀 the watched turn
spin · watch heading() · stop at the target — while


🔁 loops, nested
for draws the shape, while lands the corner — 360 ÷ sides


🚨 the open case
it printed 94 — guilty party: momentum. Diagnosed, not cured




🤔 Shape Shifter’s traces told the same story: every corner a few degrees hot, and the octagon paid 8 times. Today the coast meets its cure.

⏱️ Time: 10 minutes

title: The Drive That Heals Itself
section: Setup — same bench, one new import
text:
Setup — same bench, one new import
from pybricks.hubs import PrimeHub
from pybricks.pupdevices import Motor
from pybricks.parameters import Port, Direction
from pybricks.tools import wait, StopWatch

hub = PrimeHub()
left = Motor(Port.A, Direction.COUNTERCLOCKWISE)
right = Motor(Port.E)

# your watched turn from yesterday goes here — it still coasts to ~94. For now.

Everyone starts from their own D3 file — imports plus their watched-turn lines. StopWatch sneaks into the imports here; it gets used (and explained in one breath) in the race lab. Recap by asking, not telling: “walk me through your watched turn line by line — and who was guilty for the 94?” — two or three students, one loop. Do NOT preview the fix; the next act builds it from the coast they already diagnosed.

title: The Drive That Heals Itself
text:
Feel the fix: arrive slowly
🚗 that's your robot seen from above — nose up = heading 0°. The judge calls: turn right to the 90° tick.

  
    🚀 Full speed all the way
    
      
      
      90°
      
      
        
        
        
        
        
        
        
      
    
    0° 
  
  
    ▶ Turn to 90°
  
  
    🐢 Fast, then crawl

Same target. Same gyro. The only difference: the 🐢 arrives slowly — so there’s nothing left to coast. Fast when far, slow when close.

title: The Drive That Heals Itself
text:
The fix, in code
def gyro_turn(target):                        # def = name a move once, use it forever
    hub.imu.reset_heading(0)
    left.run(200); right.run(-200)            # fast for the long haul…
    while hub.imu.heading() < target - 30:
        wait(5)
    left.run(50); right.run(-50)              # …crawl the last 30°
    while hub.imu.heading() < target:
        wait(5)
    left.stop(); right.stop()

gyro_turn(90)     # or gyro_turn(45), gyro_turn(180)…


Two speeds, two whiles: fast for the long haul, crawl the last 30° — momentum arrives with nothing left to steal. And line 1: def names the move once; every call replays it.



First def of the bootcamp — land it hard: a function is a named move, exactly like run_angle is someone else’s named move. This version handles right turns (positive targets); if a kid asks about left turns, brilliant — flip the two run signs and the < to >, or park it as a challenge. The constants 200/50/30 are the raw material the afternoon interrogates; RobotConfig at D10. Demo on the next slide — walk the highlights here first.

title: The Drive That Heals Itself

One recipe card, many calls 📝

    gyro_turn(90)
    gyro_turn(180)

        fast … then crawl
        stop at the target

      calls made: 0 · recipes written: 1
    
  




The 94 is cured — and you just wrote your first robot move: one recipe card, any number of calls, and the 🚗 parks on every tick.




Can you beat two speeds? Imagine speed shrinking as the degrees-left shrink. Hold that thought — it comes due before lunch. 😏



Run the demo BEFORE any fragment: click all three calls, watch the same recipe card flash and the car park on three different ticks — that is the def/call story, animated. Only then reveal the cards. The teal teaser plants the smarter fix (push proportional to distance-left) — do NOT name it: it gets built and named before lunch as the start of the PID story.

title: The Drive That Heals Itself
text:
🐛 Bug hunt: the silent robot

No red error. No crash. The robot just… sits there. The code looks perfect. What did we forget?

def gyro_turn(target):
    hub.imu.reset_heading(0)
    left.run(200); right.run(-200)
    while hub.imu.heading() < target:
        wait(5)
    left.stop(); right.stop()


def only writes the recipe — it never cooks it. Nothing happens until you call the move by name: add gyro_turn(90) below. Defining a move and using a move are two separate steps.



The most instructive bug of the week — no error to read, so the robot’s stillness is the message. Elicit first: “if the code is right, why no movement?” Draw the split on the board — def …: writes the recipe card, gyro_turn(90) hands it to the cook. Leads straight into the bench lab, where they call it three times.

title: The Drive That Heals Itself
text:
Bench check: tune your crawl 🎯

Build gyro_turn, then judge-call yourself: 90° · 45° · 180° — print the landing each time.
Tune 200 / 50 / 30 until 90° lands within a degree or two. Write your three numbers on a sticky note — they’re about to testify.

⏱️ Time: 20 minutes

Perfect Pivot, folded into a bench lab: the cure is verified on 90°, but nobody is asked (yet) whether one tuning serves all three calls — that tension is the next act’s job. Make every squad physically write their three numbers down; the race lab reads them straight back.

title: The Drive That Heals Itself
text:
Three calls, one sticky note ⏱️

The judge calls 45° → 90° → 180° — same gyro_turn, same sticky note, and today the clock counts too. Two predictions with your partner:
🐢 Sleepy check: the crawl always covers the last 30°. Which call spends the biggest slice of its turn crawling — what does that do to its time?
🚀 Fly-past check: you crank the fast speed to beat the clock. Which call now risks blowing past its tick — and why that one?


  Hint: each bar is one turn, start → 🎯. Mark where the crawl starts (target − 30) on each.
  
    45°🎯crawl starts at __°
    90°🎯crawl starts at 60°
    180°🎯crawl starts at __°
  


Sketch it, then write both predictions down — the stopwatch grades them. 👀


Pure prediction, nothing revealed — the bars are deliberately unshaded; 90° is pre-filled as the worked example, squads fill 15 and 150 themselves. The sketch gives it away for 45: crawl from 15° means two-thirds of the turn is crawl — sleepy. For 180 the zone is proportionally tiny — fine at 200 deg/s, but crank the speed and the robot arrives at the zone hot: 30° isn’t enough braking distance. Don’t say any of that — take answers to both checks on the board and let the race lab grade them.

title: The Drive That Heals Itself
text:
Lab: race your own turn 🏁
timer = StopWatch()                 # a stopwatch, in code

for target in [45, 90, 180]:
    timer.reset()
    gyro_turn(target)
    elapsed = timer.time()
    wait(1000)                      # let the coast settle before you trust heading()
    print(target, "->", elapsed, "ms, landed", hub.imu.heading())
    wait(3000)                      # set the robot straight again

Run it with your bench tuning — the sticky note. Look at 45°’s time. Sleepy? That’s the crawl lane eating two-thirds of a small turn.
Now chase the clock: crank the fast speed 200 → 500 and rerun. Look at where 180° lands. 😬



One tuning, three calls: accurate but sleepy — or fast but flying past. The fixed crawl zone can’t serve every angle and every speed.



Board the results next to the predictions. Expected shape: at 200, everything lands within a few degrees but 45 feels absurdly slow for such a small turn and 180 takes ~2 s; at 500, the 180 blows through the 30° zone still hot and coasts well past (the 45 mostly survives — its crawl lane absorbs the speed). Battery level moves these numbers — that’s a feature, mention it. Squads that finish early: try 90 at 500 too.

title: The Drive That Heals Itself
text:
The tuning treadmill 🎡

Fix it with yesterday’s toolbox: a wider crawl zone? A third speed? A different tuning per angle? — what does each one cost?
Talk it out. Follow each fix two steps ahead.


Board your fixes — then we test-drive every single one. 🚗

⏱️ Time: 20 minutes (discussion + test-drives)

This is the morning’s teal teaser (“imagine speed shrinking as the degrees-left shrink”) coming due. Let them propose the staircase fixes seriously — each is a real idea with a real cost; the point isn’t that they’re dumb, it’s that they don’t scale. Collect the fixes on the board first; the next slide drives them.

Every fix adds a step and a knob — and every drive still ends the same: the 🚗 crosses the cliff still moving. What you actually want: speed that shrinks by itself as the degrees-left shrink.



How to read the picture — say it out loud, every time: the yellow dot is the robot’s moment: how far it walks right = degrees used up; how high it sits = how hard the wheels push. Apply a fix, hit ▶ drive it, and narrate the dot + car together: “far away → high up → car whips around… close → low → car creeps.” Wider zone: slower time, same coast. Extra speed: better time, same coast. Table: same staircase, ×10 retuning. The landing readout says ~92-and-something every single run — THAT’s the cliff no staircase removes. Someone usually says the magic sentence (“just make the speed depend on how far is left”) — hand them the chalk.

title: The Drive That Heals Itself
text:
You already run this controller 🚶






The game


Stand three big steps from the wall. Walk to it and stop with your toes exactly on the line. Round 1: normal. Round 2: robot style — full speed, then slam the brakes at the end. Round 3: crawl the entire way. Which round felt right — and what were your legs doing in it?





Your legs already run the deal: far → fast · close → gentle · there → zero. No zones, no steps — your speed simply follows the distance left. Robots can sign the same deal.


⏱️ Time: 5 minutes

Same unplugged-first habit as the D3 inner-ear game. Round 2 reproduces the coast (they’ll lean past the line or stutter-brake); round 3 is the sleepy 45. Ask round-1 walkers to narrate their own speed out loud — “fast… slower… slower… stop” — that sentence is the controller.

title: The Drive That Heals Itself
text:
Say the deal in numbers

Target 90°, heading reads 20°. Call the degrees-left the error — here, 70. Build the wheel speed from the error: you’re allowed one multiplication.
What does your rule give at error 70? At 10? At 0?



  speed = error × gain  (gain = 4)
  error 70 → speed 280  flat out 🚀
  error 10 → speed 40    tiptoe 🐢
  error 0  → speed 0     parks itself 🎯




speed = error × gain — the whole staircase, in one line. gain is the slope: how hard to push per degree still to go. And at error 0 the speed is 0 — it doesn’t need to be told to stop.



Elicit the multiplication — don’t reveal it. Most squads propose “speed = distance left” first; ask “what if the wheels need bigger numbers?” and the × gain falls out. The three sample rows are the wall-walk in numbers: narrate them as “fast… slower… stop”, same sentence as the game.

Every staircase fix added a step. The ramp has no steps to tune — it re-measures the error 200×/second and touches zero exactly at the target, so there’s never anything left to coast.



Same reading ritual as the test-drive slide — the kids already know it: dot walks right = degrees getting used up; dot height = wheel push; the 🚗 dial is the same robot in real life. Drive the staircase first and narrate; then drive the ramp. Freeze the comparison at error = 30: the ramp is still doing 120 where the staircase crawls at 50 — faster there, yet it parks softer, because it keeps shrinking all the way in. That resolves the “fast OR accurate” trade from the race lab. The bars under the dial are the two axes, live — point at bar ↔︎ axis once and the graph is decoded.

title: The Drive That Heals Itself
text:
The proportional turn
def gyro_turn_p(target, gain=4):
    hub.imu.reset_heading(0)
    while abs(target - hub.imu.heading()) > 1:   # until within 1°…
        error = target - hub.imu.heading()       # degrees still to go
        left.run(gain * error)                   # far → fast
        right.run(-gain * error)                 # close → gentle
        wait(5)
    left.stop(); right.stop()

gyro_turn_p(90)   # gyro_turn_p(45)… gyro_turn_p(180)… gyro_turn_p(-90)!


Line 10: a negative target flips the sign, the wheels swap — left turns come free. Yesterday’s version needed surgery for that.



Walk the highlight stops: gain arrives as a parameter with a default — new syntax, teach it in one breath (“a knob you can leave alone”). Line 3’s abs() is what makes negative targets work. Demo on the next slide.
Coach aside — “why run() and not motor.dc()?” (for the sharp student who’s read ahead). Honest truth: run() is itself a closed-loop speed controller inside Pybricks — so this hand-written P sits on top of it, a controller-on-a-controller. Your gain sets a speed target; Pybricks’ own loop chases it. A pro writing a controller from scratch often reaches for motor.dc() instead — raw power/duty — so their formula is the whole controller, one layer, nothing hidden underneath. Totally legitimate, and purer. We keep run() on purpose for two reasons: it’s smoother and battery-proof (a fresh vs. tired hub turns the same — priceless with five robots on the bench), and it speaks the same “speed” language as drive.drive(speed, turn) and DriveBase — so speed = error × gain stays literally true all the way from this turn, through the self-healing straight, to D7’s line-follower. dc() is the right tool the day you build a controller with no help underneath; that day isn’t this bootcamp.

title: The Drive That Heals Itself
text:
Playground: one knob, every angle 🎛️

  
    judge calls:
    ▶ 45°
    ▶ 90°

    the game: land within ±2° and under 1.3 s — on all three calls. Which side can?

Drive it from their hypotheses. Round 1: all three calls at fast = 200 — the staircase lands everything but watch 180’s clock (~2 s) and how long 45 spends in the crawl lane. Round 2: crank fast to 600 to beat the clock — 180 blows past by ~7°: the fixed zone can’t absorb the speed. Round 3: the P side at gain 4 — all three calls land within a degree, at staircase-500 pace, same knob. Then let them feel the failures: gain 1 parks short (friction beats a tiny push — tomorrow’s breadcrumb), gain 20 arrives hot and flies past. The sim is illustrative, not gospel — the real robot’s sweet spot is theirs to find in the lab.

title: The Drive That Heals Itself
text:
Name the move 🏷️
You just built: a controller whose push is proportional to the error.

the P-controller
P = proportional — the first letter of PID



This exact line runs inside every drone, thermostat, cruise control, elevator, and Mars rover. You didn’t learn a LEGO trick today — you wrote the opening letter of the most-used formula in robotics. I and D? They earn their seats tomorrow.



This is the payoff of yesterday’s “it has a name every roboticist on Earth knows” — let it land with a beat of silence. Do NOT preview what I and D do; tomorrow each arrives as the rescue to a failure they’ll see (P in a hurry overshoots → D; a leftover P can’t kill → I).

title: The Drive That Heals Itself
text:
Quick quiz: the gain knob 🚨

Before you touch the robot: gain = 1 — what will the turn feel like? gain = 20? Predict both.



1 = lazy — near the target it pushes too weakly to beat friction and parks short. 20 = hot — it flies past and zig-zags back. Tuning is the middle: hard enough to arrive, soft enough to arrive calm. That lazy leftover it parks on gets a name tomorrow. 😏



The prediction is the day’s real gate: “the bigger the error, the harder the push” plus what too-small/too-big does. Collect predictions out loud, then feel the answers — flick back to the playground and drag the gain slider to 1 and 20, or better, straight onto the bench robot in the lab. Per the plan, tuning this by feel without prompting is the strongest robotics-instinct signal on the scorecard — watch for who reaches for the knob before you suggest it.

title: The Drive That Heals Itself
text:
Lab: your gain, your rematch 🎛️

Build gyro_turn_p, then rerun the morning race — same three calls, one gain. Beat your two-speed times and landings?
Find your gain: start at 4 → halve it, double it, feel both failures, close in from the middle.
Try gyro_turn_p(-90). No surgery required. 😎

⏱️ Time: 30 minutes

Every squad’s best gain is different (battery, tire wear, robot mass) — that’s the point of it being a knob, and why nobody copies a neighbor’s number. Log times + landings like the morning so the win over the staircase is on paper. Fast squads: call gyro_turn_p(360) — a full spin that parks where it started — or race 45 at gain 8 vs 4.

title: The Drive That Heals Itself
text:
Break it: the nudge test 💥
hub.imu.reset_heading(0)
left.reset_angle(0)              # zero the wheel's degree-counter

left.run(500); right.run(500)
while left.angle() < 1304:       # ≈ 100 cm — Day 2's number!
    print(hub.imu.heading())
    wait(100)
left.stop(); right.stop()

left.angle() = the same degree-counter run_angle uses — now you read it.
While it drives, give the robot’s side a gentle nudge (or lay a pencil in its path). Watch the printout…


Line-walk the code first; the sim of the nudge is on the next slide, then they do it for real on the bench.

title: The Drive That Heals Itself
text:
Talk it out

Heading says +8 — nosed right. You command each wheel’s speed. What should the wheels do about it?
And should a +2 error get the same medicine as a +20 error?



Steer back left — right wheel faster, left slower — until heading reads 0. And the bigger the error, the harder the push.

title: The Drive That Heals Itself
text:
The drive that heals itself
hub.imu.reset_heading(0)
left.reset_angle(0)

while left.angle() < 1304:        # until ≈ 100 cm rolls by…
    error = hub.imu.heading()     # +8 = nosed right · -5 = nosed left
    left.run(500 - 4 * error)     # nosed right? left eases off…
    right.run(500 + 4 * error)    # …right pushes harder → steers back left
    wait(10)
left.stop(); right.stop()
Run it — and nudge it again. 😈


It fights back. A hundred times a second: read the error, steer against it. Error 0 → wheels equal → dead straight. That tiny loop is the heart of every self-driving machine on Earth.



Check the signs with them, don’t just assert: error +8 → left runs 468, right runs 532 → right faster → robot swings left → error shrinks. Negative error works out symmetrically. The 4 is a strength knob — let one squad try 1 (lazy) and 20 (jittery zig-zag). Name it out loud: the same P-controller as this morning’s turn — one formula, second application; the third is D7’s line-follower. Same run()-is-a-speed-controller caveat as the proportional-turn note applies here — the 500 ± 4*error is a base speed plus a proportional trim, and it stays on run() for the same reasons (smooth, battery-proof, speaks DriveBase’s “speed” language).

title: The Drive That Heals Itself
text:
Nudge Wars 💥






The challenge


Drive 1.5 m straight while the judge nudges your robot twice mid-run. Score = closest final heading to 0° + straightest trace. 3 attempts, best counts.




Tune on the bench first — your straight’s 4 is a knob too: too soft and a shove sends you wandering; too hot and the trace zig-zags all the way down the lane.
Watch one recovery closely: the harder the shove, the harder the fight-back. The shove is the error.


Format: a 1.5 m taped lane; the judge nudges at roughly the 0.5 m and 1.0 m marks with a consistent finger-push to the robot’s side. Final |heading| is the score; trace straightness (eyeball the wheel-path) breaks ties. Uses only today’s material: error × gain on the straight. This competition is the plan’s flagged talent signal — P-tuning instinct is the strongest predictor of robotics skill; note on the scorecard who re-tunes between attempts by feel (gain reasoning: lazy vs jittery), not by guessing.

title: The Drive That Heals Itself
text:
Today’s gate






You’re done today when…


One gain turns your robot to any called angle — both directions — with your own gyro_turn_p; your 1.5 m straight fights back through two nudges; and you can say “the bigger the error, the harder the push” and predict what a too-small / too-big gain does before running it.




Tomorrow: put P in a hurry and watch it slam past the target — meet the letter that damps it. Then a lean P never quite kills — one more letter. Finish the acronym… and start naming moves in a library of your own. 📦


The gate is the plan’s D4 outcome verbatim: turn to any angle + hold heading through nudges with a hand-written P, plus the gain prediction in their own words — same formula, two applications today (turn + straight); the third is D7’s line. The teaser sets up D5 without naming D or I; D5 finishes the acronym and begins my_lib.py (drive_straight/turn_to) — the DriveBase surprise now waits at the D10 reveal (decision #20), earned only after the whole library is theirs.
